The chunking knob is configurable, but the default chunking policy ('set empirically via experiments') is deferred to experiment results not yet present in the plan. There is no stated guard against per-ticket LLM-call count growing with the number of files/criteria in a way that is unbounded relative to ticket size (potential O(files × criteria) pattern depending on per-file LSP calls).

(c) SCALABILITY — Concurrency/rate-limit handling for the LLM calls and the LSP server under simultaneous claims by multiple agents is not mentioned. Registry lookups (syft + package registry) introduce external I/O with no timeout/retry/circuit-breaker statement beyond 'fail-open on crash/timeout.' The centrality/blast-radius computation 'at plan time' has no stated complexity bound or caching strategy.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated 'Performance contract' sub-section (or expand the Execution bullet) that states: (1) an explicit latency SLO for the claim fast-path (e.g., <200 ms), (2) a wall-clock budget ceiling for the out-of-band review (e.g., ≤N minutes before INDETERMINATE is emitted), (3) explicit confirmation that zero synchronous LLM/LSP calls occur on the claim hot path, (4) a per-ticket ceiling on total LLM calls (e.g., max M chunks × K criteria), (5) timeout + retry policy for external registry/LSP calls (already partly addressed by fail-open, but make timeouts explicit), and (6) concurrency model (e.g., one LSP server instance per language per review worker, rate-limited queue for LLM calls).

findings.location: Overall plan structure; Follow-on work section (F1–F12); AC list (12 enumerated items); Design summary
Acceptance Criteria — all ACs; Success Criteria SC3; Design summary 'Two layers' section
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan whatsoever — neither for Layer-1 deterministic checks nor for Layer-2 advisory criteria, nor for the claim integration, attestation signing, sidecar event, or config surface. Specifically: (a) No failure/timeout/invalid/empty-path test cases are named anywhere (e.g., what happens when solidlsp crashes mid-review, when the LLM returns malformed JSON, when the budget is exhausted mid-chunk, when the attestation signature is tampered). (b) No boundary scenarios are enumerated (e.g., oversized ticket description, ticket with no description, non-Latin content in ACs, a ticket whose file_impact list is empty vs. absent). (c) Stated outcomes are not observable/measurable — SC3 says findings 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' but no measurement mechanism, baseline, or test assertion is described. (d) No TDD specification: no named RED tests, no dependency edges declared for any unit. (e) No mention of modifying or removing existing tests for changed behaviors (e.g., the claim flow is being changed — existing claim tests presumably need updating). (f) No assertion strategy is described at all — it is entirely unclear whether any automated test suite will cover this feature. The plan is a large, novel, user-facing flow (claim gate + signing + sidecar + config + routing + orchestrator + two layers of criteria) with zero testing plan.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated '## Testing Plan' section to the epic covering at minimum: (1) Unit tests for each Layer-1 check with named failure paths (tool crash → PASS/skip, LSP timeout → PASS/skip, malformed tool output → PASS/skip). (2) Integration tests for the claim flow: valid attestation passes, absent attestation blocks, stale (material-edit-invalidated) attestation blocks, --force override records justification. (3) Contract/regression tests for attestation signing and staleness detection (tampered signature, no material-event-commit, edit after signing). (4) Tests for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event: event is emitted, reducer skips it, fields sufficient for offline analysis. (5) Named RED tests for at least the claim integration and one Layer-1 check. (6) Explicit policy for what happens to existing claim-flow tests.

findings.location: N/A
Acceptance Criteria — all items; Success Criteria — all items; no dedicated testing section in the plan body
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan at all — not a single test case, test type, RED/GREEN cycle, or testing strategy is described anywhere. This is a plan for a new user-facing gate that intercepts the open→in_progress transition, emits signed attestations, integrates with `claim`, writes `REVIEW_RESULT` events, and invokes external tools (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, lizard). None of the following are addressed: (a) happy-path coverage — no mention of e.g. 'a ticket with a valid signature passes claim'; (b) failure/timeout/invalid paths — e.g. what happens when the tool crashes, when the attestation is stale, when signing fails, when budget is exceeded; (c) boundary scenarios — e.g. oversized ticket descriptions, non-Latin content in plan text, empty AC lists; (d) TDD is entirely absent — no RED test is named for any unit; (e) no test work is described for changed behavior (the `claim` command path is modified but no corresponding test is identified); (f) no assertion strategy is described (unit isolation of LLM calls, mocking of external tools, integration test of the gate end-to-end). The only testing-adjacent language in the plan is 'experiment results' referenced in AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' — but this refers to calibration data, not a test suite.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated 'Testing strategy' section (or expand each AC item) covering at minimum: (1) Unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks — name at least one RED test per check (e.g. `test_file_resolution_missing_symbol_returns_FAIL`, `test_oversize_signal_fail_open_on_unsupported_lang`); (2) Unit tests for the attestation/signing subsystem — stale signature detection, material-edit invalidation, coverage record roundtrip; (3) Integration test of the `claim` gate path — valid sig passes, absent sig blocks, `--force` override logs justification; (4) Failure/timeout paths for every external tool — tool crash → skipped = PASS, timeout → skipped = PASS; (5) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar — assert the reducer skips the event type and the emitted payload contains required fingerprint fields; (6) Layer-2 advisory output contract — assert coaching output is grounded/ranked/checklist-form (can use fixture-based LLM output tests). Cite whether TDD applies or cite an exemption (e.g. pure orchestration scaffolding).

findings.location: N/A

Design summary — 'Two layers', 'Criteria registry', 'Attestation', 'claim integration', 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event'; AC items: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points', 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check', 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'
findings.finding:
The plan clearly crosses multiple component boundaries (rebar.llm, claim/open→in_progress transition, the reducer/event store, the completion-verifier signing subsystem, solidlsp/ast-grep tooling) and introduces several new pipeline stages and contracts. Three sub-checks apply:

(a) coupling_risk — AMBIGUOUS. The plan acknowledges reuse of rebar.llm extension points and the completion-verifier signing subsystem, and places REVIEW_RESULT as a reducer-ignored sidecar to stay off hot paths. However, the specific interface/event boundary between the new orchestrator and the existing claim flow is not spelled out. It is unclear whether the claim check calls into a new service, reads a head-state event, or calls a shared library function. Without that, the coupling risk of the fast-path claim check is not explicitly mitigated.

(b) changeability — PASS. Rules/thresholds expected to evolve (chunking, budget caps, per-criterion blocking, depth routing, escalation) are explicitly called out as project-configurable knobs. The criteria registry is declarative and versioned. No hardcoded thresholds are apparent.

(c) documentation — MINOR finding. The plan introduces a novel pipeline stage (open→in_progress gate), a new signing/attestation contract (extending the close-gate signing), a new event type (REVIEW_RESULT), and a new orchestration pattern (criteria registry + generic orchestrator). The plan references a session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') as the design-of-record but does not commit to producing an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update as part of this epic. The 'Docs' AC item covers user-facing docs (how the gate works, config surface, cert semantics) but does not explicitly call for an architectural decision record capturing the novel pipeline stage, the signing contract extension, or the sidecar-event pattern.
findings.suggested_edit: In the Acceptance Criteria 'Docs' item, explicitly add: 'An ADR (or equivalent CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) capturing: (1) the open→in_progress gate as a new pipeline stage and its interface with the claim flow, (2) the extension of the close-gate signing contract, and (3) the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar-event pattern and its reducer-skip contract.' Also, consider naming the concrete interface boundary (e.g., 'claim reads the latest PLAN_REVIEW_ATTESTATION head-state event') so coupling_risk is unambiguous.

findings.location: N/A
findings.finding: The plan does not add any network endpoints, user-facing data access paths, PII handling, or new auth boundaries. The attestation signing extends an existing internal mechanism (the close-gate signing subsystem), the REVIEW_RESULT event is an internal reducer-ignored sidecar, and the CI-check signal is described as a readable head-state event — none of these constitute a new endpoint or sensitive data path requiring OWASP-baseline analysis. The security overlay criterion is not applicable.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding:
Several acceptance criteria defer concrete values to external artifacts that are not reproduced in the plan:
1. Chunking defaults, budget caps, and depth thresholds are deferred to 'experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — the linked session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) is not accessible to a reviewing agent, so the executor cannot determine what values to use without stopping to retrieve or re-derive them.
2. 'Centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' (Design summary + AC) — the algorithm or data source for centrality is not specified (LSP call-graph? import graph depth? something else?). An agent would need to make an architectural choice.
3. 'A project can configure risk posture … with sensible defaults shipped' (Success Criteria 4) — the specific config keys, their types, and their default values are not listed anywhere in the plan body, only named abstractly.
4. The criteria registry format ('declarative descriptor') is referenced but its schema/fields are not defined, leaving the executor to invent it.
None of these individually block the plan completely (an agent can make reasonable defaults and document them), but item 1 (experiment-derived defaults) is the most exposed: the AC explicitly requires the defaults to be grounded in specific experiment data, yet that data is only accessible via an opaque session-log reference.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the key empirical values (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per LLM call; per-plan budget cap = $X; depth tiers = [low/medium/high] mapped to complexity score thresholds A/B') derived from the experiments, or add an explicit sub-task: 'Run experiments E1–E3 and record results in config/plan_review_defaults.yaml before implementing AC item 11.' Similarly, name the centrality metric and its source (e.g., 'import-graph in-degree from ast-grep') so the executor does not have to choose.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; full plan body
AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'
findings.finding:
Several design parameters are deferred to external artifacts or experiments that are not self-contained in the plan:

1. **Experiment-derived defaults (AC bullet 11):** 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).' The experiments are not linked in the plan text; the executor must locate session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` and locate the relevant results. If those experiments have not yet been run or are not findable, the executor is left to choose defaults without guidance — a real gap.

2. **Centrality/blast-radius computation (AC bullet 6):** 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' — no definition of what centrality metric to use (e.g., PageRank on import graph? number of callers? something else?), no threshold that triggers escalation. An agent implementing this would need to make an unconstrained design choice.

3. **Criteria chunking knob (AC bullet 6):** 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB' — the default chunk size is stated to come from experiments, but no fallback default is named for an executor who cannot access those results.

4. **Per-plan budget cap (AC bullet 6):** Budget cap is mentioned but no unit, order of magnitude, or formula is given. An executor cannot implement a budget cap without a concrete value or derivation rule.

5. **'Triggered overlays (router-selected)' (AC bullet 2):** What overlays exist, what routing conditions trigger them, and what criteria they add are not described anywhere in the plan body. This is a non-trivial feature surface with no actionable spec.

None of these individually blocks the bulk of the implementation, and the plan is otherwise highly detailed. However, signals 1, 3, and 4 together mean an executor cannot ship production-ready defaults without stopping to investigate external artifacts or make unconstrained choices.
findings.suggested_edit: For each deferred parameter, add either (a) a concrete default value/range directly in the AC (e.g., 'default chunk size = 3 criteria per call; revisable post-experiment'), or (b) an explicit decision rule the executor can apply without accessing external artifacts (e.g., 'if experiment results are unavailable, default budget cap = N tokens per chunk'). For centrality, name the specific metric (e.g., 'number of direct importers via ast-grep/LSP call graph'). For triggered overlays, add a sub-bullet listing at least the overlay names and their trigger conditions.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design summary; Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria

Design summary — 'Two layers', 'Criteria registry', 'Attestation', 'Execution', 'Observability / FP substrate'; Acceptance Criteria — items on orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT event
findings.finding:
The plan clearly crosses multiple component boundaries — it adds a new pipeline stage (open→in_progress gate), a criteria registry with a generic orchestrator reusing rebar.llm extension points, a new attestation/signing subsystem extending the close-gate signing, a `claim` integration point, and a reducer-ignored sidecar event type (`REVIEW_RESULT`). The maintainability overlay therefore applies in full.

(a) Coupling risk: Several new cross-component dependencies are introduced — the review gate hooks into the `claim` command/flow, the attestation subsystem is said to 'extend the close-gate signing', and the orchestrator reuses 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'. These integrations are named but not justified or mitigated with explicit interface/event boundaries in the plan. For example, how the orchestrator couples to rebar.llm (import, API contract, shared event bus?) is not specified. Whether the claim-check coupling is mediated by an interface or is a direct call is unstated. This is AMBIGUOUS without codebase access — the integrations may already be well-bounded in the existing code; we cannot confirm.

(b) Changeability: Several thresholds and knobs are described as configurable (chunking, budget caps, per-criterion blocking, depth routing, INDETERMINATE blocking) and defaults are said to be 'grounded in experiment results'. This is good. However, the 'material-edit' definition (what events invalidate attestation: 'description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edits invalidate; tags/comments/links/assignee do not') is a business rule baked into the design summary with no mention of where it lives in config or code — if the boundary between 'material' and 'immaterial' events needs adjustment, it is unclear whether this requires a code change or is configurable. This is a minor changeability risk.

(c) Documentation: The plan references a session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) as the 'plan-of-record v4' and states 'Full design detail lives in session log'. The AC includes a 'Docs' item covering gate mechanics, config surface, fail-open behavior, cert semantics, and CI-check pattern — which is good. However, there is no mention of an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or architectural design doc for the novel decisions introduced: (i) the two-layer verdict model as a new architectural pattern, (ii) the sidecar event approach for observability, (iii) the signing/binding strategy for attestations. The 'Docs' AC item reads as user-facing documentation, not an internal architectural decision record. This is a gap on sub-check (c): a new pipeline stage and novel architectural decisions (sidecar event, two-layer gate, signing scheme) lack an explicit ADR/CLAUDE.md update requirement in the plan.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Add an explicit AC item: 'An ADR or CLAUDE.md section is added capturing: (i) the two-layer gate architecture and verdict model, (ii) the sidecar REVIEW_RESULT event pattern and its reducer-ignored status, (iii) the attestation signing and material-event-commit binding scheme, and (iv) how the orchestrator couples to rebar.llm (interface contract).'
2. Clarify whether the 'material vs immaterial edit' classification is configurable or hardcoded, and if the latter, note it in the ADR as a deliberate frozen rule.
3. For each cross-component integration (claim hook, rebar.llm orchestrator, close-gate attestation extension), add a sentence naming the interface boundary or contract used (e.g., 'via existing rebar.llm PromptRunner protocol', 'via a new ClaimGate interface').

findings.location: Design summary — 'Attestation': 'signed result', 'material event commit binding'; Acceptance Criteria — 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'; 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event with finding fingerprints'
findings.finding:
The plan introduces signed attestations, a `--force` override with justification logging, and a `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event containing per-criterion verdicts, finding fingerprints, and override justifications. It also mentions the attestation is 'checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal'. These constitute new data storage/transmission paths and an access/auth boundary consideration that triggers the security overlay.

(a) Access classification: The plan does not declare the access level of the attestation-check endpoint or CI-check integration point. The `claim` check is described as a 'fast check for a valid signed attestation' but whether this is public, authenticated, or internal-only is unstated. The CI signal path (external CI process reading the attestation) similarly has no declared access model. This is AMBIGUOUS — the existing `claim` flow may already have an established auth boundary in the codebase, and if so the new check inherits it; we cannot confirm without code access. If the CI integration point is a new external-facing API or token-checked endpoint, it would need explicit access classification (OWASP A01 Broken Access Control).

(b) Data protection: The `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event captures 'finding fingerprints, metadata, and override justifications'. The plan does not specify what 'override justifications' may contain (could include free-text agent reasoning about why a check was bypassed — potentially sensitive). It also does not address whether `--force` justification text is sanitized before storage. The attestation signing scheme is mentioned but the signing key management approach (where keys live, rotation, access control) is not addressed. These are minor gaps — the system appears to be internal tooling rather than a public-facing service, which limits exposure, but key management for the signing component is a real (if minor) concern. No PII appears to be in scope.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Add a sentence to the Attestation AC item explicitly declaring the access model: e.g., 'The attestation endpoint/check is internal-only (no unauthenticated external access); the CI integration reads the signature via [token/artifact/read-only role].'
2. Add a note on signing key management: 'Signing keys are stored in [secrets manager / env var / rebar config]; rotation procedure is documented.'
3. Clarify whether `--force` justification text is free-form and, if so, note any sanitization or length-cap applied before it is stored in the REVIEW_RESULT event.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)'; Design summary; Acceptance Criteria

Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp … syft … scc + lizard'; 'Execution: … chunk-atomic checkpointing'; 'Attestation: signed result … bound to the latest MATERIAL event commit'; AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple external failure points (LSP servers, ast-grep, syft, registry lookups, LLM calls) and stateful write operations (emitting the signed attestation event, the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event, chunk-atomic checkpointing). The fail-open ('skipped = PASS') policy is stated clearly for tooling, which handles (a) graceful degradation for tool crashes/timeouts. However: (1) error handling for LLM calls — retry/backoff/circuit-breaker — is not mentioned anywhere in the plan; LLM failures are only addressed as 'INDETERMINATE (non-blocking default)', but no guidance is given on transient vs. terminal LLM errors, or on surfacing those errors rather than silently producing INDETERMINATE. (2) Idempotency/safety of the signed attestation write and the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event on partial failure (e.g., the process dies mid-review, or the event store rejects the write) is not addressed. Chunk-atomic checkpointing is mentioned but its durability semantics and recovery path on crash are not described. Whether these gaps are filled in the referenced session log (63cc-ec94-4792-464b) cannot be determined without codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit failure-handling section (or extend the 'Execution' and 'Attestation' bullets) that covers: (a) LLM-call retry/backoff policy and when a transient LLM error escalates to INDETERMINATE vs. is retried; (b) idempotency guarantee for the attestation and REVIEW_RESULT event writes — e.g., event-store write is compare-and-swap or append-only with deduplication; (c) what 'chunk-atomic checkpointing' means in terms of durability (written to durable store before proceeding?) and the recovery path if the process crashes mid-review.

findings.location: Design summary; Acceptance Criteria (12 enumerated items); Follow-on work (F1–F12)

Attestation section: 'signed result … composable CI rigor signal'; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'; AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a cryptographic signing mechanism and a CI-checkable attestation artifact — these are new data paths where the signing key material must be stored and accessed. The plan does not declare how signing keys are managed (where they live, who/what can access them, rotation policy, secret-logging risk). Additionally, the '--force override with justification' path writes override justifications to the REVIEW_RESULT event; if any ticket or plan content includes PII or sensitive data, that could be captured in the event store. These are not clearly in-scope data-storage/transmission paths, so the finding is AMBIGUOUS rather than a definitive FAIL — the session log may cover this, and the severity depends on the broader rebar architecture's existing secret-management patterns which are not visible here.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Key management' note under the Attestation section: specify where the signing key is stored (e.g., rebar config, secrets manager), who can read it, and confirm no key material or sensitive content is captured in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event or override justification log.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all 12 bullet points; Success Criteria — all 6 bullet points
findings.finding:
No acceptance criterion maps to a concrete proving command or check that would produce evidence of success. The ACs describe what must be 'implemented' or 'emitted' (e.g., 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented', 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented', 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding', 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event') but never specify how to verify that the implementation is correct and complete — there is no 'run X, observe Y' test for any individual criterion.

Specific gaps:
1. AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override' — no proving command (e.g., 'run `rebar claim <ticket>` without attestation and confirm the blocking error; run with `--force` and confirm the justification is recorded').
2. AC: 'Layer-1 … fail-open' — no command showing that a crash/timeout in a tool produces PASS rather than FAIL.
3. AC: 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event' — no command or test asserting that the reducer skips the event type.
4. AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in experiment results' — references linked experiment work that is not part of this plan; no in-plan proving step.
5. Success Criterion: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan' — 'measurably help' is entirely unmeasured within the epic (the measurement tooling is deferred to Follow-on F1/F10).
6. Success Criterion: 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process' — no end-to-end check of the CI integration path is defined.

The union of the described steps (criteria registry, orchestrator, attestation, sidecar, claim integration, docs) does not fully close the gap to every AC: for instance, the AC about 'Docs' has no acceptance test, and the AC about 'per-criterion config: advisory | blocking-with-override' has no proving step at all.

Hedge language is present in the design ('measurably help an agent revise', 'sensible defaults shipped', 'sufficient to reconstruct') without paired proving commands.
findings.suggested_edit:
For each AC, add a subsection 'Verification:' with a concrete command or automated test, e.g.:
- AC claim integration → 'Verification: `pytest tests/gate/test_claim.py::test_blocks_without_attestation` passes; `pytest tests/gate/test_claim.py::test_force_override_recorded` passes.'
- AC fail-open → 'Verification: unit test that injects a tool-crash and asserts verdict == PASS/skipped.'
- AC REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored → 'Verification: assert reducer output is identical before and after appending a REVIEW_RESULT event to the event log.'
- Remove or replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' from the Success Criteria, or move the measurement to a concrete in-session metric (e.g., 'at least one end-to-end integration test scenario shows a plan with a known defect receiving a FAIL finding that, when addressed, clears to PASS').

findings.location: Success Criteria — SC3; SC4; SC6; Acceptance Criteria — AC11 ('Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'); AC1 ('Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented … readiness shape … file/symbol/import resolution … package existence … oversize signals … task-DAG validity … AC/DD quality … destructive sniff'); AC2 (all Layer-2 criteria in one bullet)
findings.finding:
Multiple criteria fail measurability and/or in-session completability checks:

1. **Epic-of-epics scope (F1d — not right-sized):** The plan is explicitly an 'epic' spanning at minimum: a criteria registry, a generic orchestrator, Layer-1 (8+ deterministic checks across polyglot tooling), Layer-2 (12+ advisory checks + router + overlays + broad pass), attestation/signing, claim integration, configurable chunking + depth routing + model escalation, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip, per-criterion config knob, experiment-derived defaults, and docs. These are individually non-trivial subsystems. Bundled into one ticket, no agent can complete this in a single working session.

2. **Post-sprint-only / deferred validation (F1b):** SC3 reads 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'. The measurement mechanism is explicitly deferred to Follow-on F1 (FP/remediation-rate tracking) and F10 (calibrated abstention + labeled gold set). As written there is no in-session, deterministic way to evaluate whether findings 'measurably help'.

3. **Effort-framed, not outcome-framed (F1a):** Several ACs describe work to be done rather than observable outcomes:
   - AC1: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python' — effort frame. An outcome frame would specify what the system returns given a known input.
   - AC2: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented' — same issue.
   - AC11: 'Defaults … grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — depends on external linked work; the in-session observable outcome (what the default values are and why) is not stated.

4. **Subjective / unmeasured terms (F1a):** SC4 includes 'sensible defaults shipped' — 'sensible' is subjective with no defined acceptance bar. SC6 includes 'rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal' — not quantified or testable in-session.

5. **AC11 references out-of-plan artifacts (F1b):** 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — the experiment work is a separate artifact not included in this plan; the outcome cannot be evaluated solely from this plan's repo artifacts.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. **Decompose the epic** into child tickets, each with a single-outcome AC (e.g., 'Layer-1 fail-open tooling wrapper', 'claim integration + attestation check', 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip', 'Layer-2 criterion registry + orchestrator', 'configurable chunking + depth routing'). The parent epic ticket should hold vision/design only; concrete ACs belong on children.
2. **Replace effort-framed ACs** with observable outcomes, e.g., 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented' → 'Given a ticket with a missing `## Acceptance Criteria` section, the gate returns a Layer-1 BLOCK verdict; given an unsupported language, it returns PASS/skipped — confirmed by integration tests `test_layer1_block` and `test_layer1_fail_open`'.
3. **Remove or defer SC3's measurability claim**: change 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' to 'return findings that conform to the coaching spec (grounded, specific, severity-ranked, checklist-form), verified by a rubric-based unit test on a synthetic defective plan'.
4. **Pin AC11** by including the experiment-derived default values in the plan itself (or in a linked config file committed as part of this epic), so the outcome is evaluable in-session.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Layer-1 and Layer-2 bullet lists; Success Criteria bullet 3; 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan at all for the gate itself. There is no mention of: (a) failure/timeout/invalid-input paths being tested (e.g., what happens when solidlsp crashes, when the LLM times out mid-chunk, when a ticket has no AC section, when the attestation signature is malformed, when --force is invoked without justification); (b) boundary scenarios (oversized ticket description, non-Latin content in AC, a ticket with hundreds of children); (c) observable/measurable test outcomes — no 'RED test fails before implementation' or even 'CI must pass' assertions; (d) no TDD anchors for any of the named components (criteria registry, orchestrator, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, signing/invalidation logic); (e) no mention of modifying or removing tests for changed behaviors (e.g., existing claim flow tests that must be updated when the gate is inserted); (f) no isolation strategy for external deps (LSP server, LLM calls, syft/registry) in tests. The plan defers everything to follow-on work (F1–F12) but even in-scope work has zero test specification.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing plan' section (or annotate each AC) covering at minimum: (1) Unit tests for Layer-1 checks with mocked tooling — assert fail-open on tool crash/timeout and PASS verdict emitted; (2) Integration tests for the claim path — assert that missing/stale attestation returns a blocking error and --force with justification succeeds; (3) Contract tests for REVIEW_RESULT event schema — assert reducer skips the type without error; (4) At least one named RED test per major component before implementation (e.g., 'test_claim_blocks_without_attestation' fails before AC 'claim integration' is implemented); (5) Boundary: ticket with no '## Acceptance Criteria' heading, ticket with 500+ children, non-ASCII AC text.

findings.location: Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; Design summary: 'posture is to COACH agents'; Success Criteria bullet 3 and 6
Acceptance Criteria — all 12 bullet points collectively; Success Criteria bullets 1–6
findings.finding: Several ACs fail the in-session completability and measurability checks: (a) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)' — the experiment work is referenced but not included in this epic's scope; if those experiments are not already completed and their results committed to the repo, this AC cannot be satisfied in-session and its outcome is not evaluable against any concrete target. (b) 'Layer-2 advisory criteria … measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criterion 3) — 'measurably help' is a post-sprint outcome (requires observing agent trajectories over time) and is not evaluable in-session against repo artifacts alone. (c) 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)' is subjective as written — there is no declared rubric or automated check that makes it evaluable by CI or a deterministic command. (d) The AC list is epic-of-epics scale (12 compound bullets, each containing multiple sub-deliverables), making any single-session completability claim implausible; the plan does not decompose into child tickets here.
findings.suggested_edit: For (a): either confirm the experiment tickets are already closed and link their result artifacts, or scope out the 'grounded in experiment results' qualifier and replace with an explicit default value placeholder reviewable in-session. For (b): replace 'measurably help' with an in-session proxy (e.g., 'at least one complete before/after plan revision example is included in docs demonstrating a coaching finding being addressed'). For (c): define a machine-checkable rubric (e.g., every finding must include fields: criterion_id, location, severity, suggested_edit) enforced by a schema. For (d): add a child-ticket decomposition AC or note that child tickets are pre-filed.

findings.location: Design summary: coupling/integration points; Attestation section; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'; Polyglot tooling section
Acceptance Criteria — all 12 bullet points; Success Criteria — all 6 bullet points
findings.finding:
No acceptance criterion has a concrete proving command (a runnable check that produces evidence of success). The plan describes *what* must exist but never specifies *how done-ness is verified*. Specific gaps:

(a) No proving command is given for any criterion. E.g., 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented … fail-open' — there is no test invocation, no CI target, no integration-test command that would demonstrate fail-open behaviour on an unsupported language.

(b) Red-flag hedges appear throughout: 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criterion 3) is unmeasured — no metric, no threshold, no command proves it; 'sensible defaults shipped' (SC 4) is subjective; 'sufficient to reconstruct per-criterion FP/remediation analysis offline' (AC 8 / SC 5) has no acceptance test that exercises reconstruction.

(c) Several ACs have no described step that maps to them. AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' refers to an external session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) that is not part of this plan's described steps, leaving no reachable path to satisfying it from within the epic.

(d) The gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like' is especially wide for the attestation/signing subsystem: AC says 'signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit' but there is no described step (test scenario, command, or verifiable artifact) that would confirm invalidation actually fires on a material edit.

(e) The user-facing flow (`claim` blocking on missing/stale attestation) has no end-to-end scenario or command specified — only that '`claim` integration: fast signed-attestation check' must exist.
findings.suggested_edit:
For each AC, add a 'Verified by:' sub-bullet naming a concrete command or artifact. Examples:
• Layer-1 fail-open: `pytest tests/plan_review/layer1/ -k unsupported_lang` exits 0 and logs 'SKIPPED=PASS'.
• Attestation invalidation: running `rebar claim <ticket>` after editing an AC field returns exit-code 1 with 'attestation stale'.
• `claim` gate: integration test that attempts `claim` without attestation and asserts the recorded block event.
• Drop or sharpen 'measurably help an agent revise' — replace with a measurable proxy (e.g., 'all advisory findings include criterion_id, location quote, and suggested_edit fields, verified by schema validation').
• Either include the experiment results inline or add an explicit step 'run chunking-calibration experiment; record outputs in config defaults PR' so the AC is reachable within the epic.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 12 bullets); Success Criteria (all 6 bullets); Follow-on work section
findings.finding:
Multiple criteria fail measurability and/or in-session completability checks:

(a) Effort-framed / outcome-vague criteria:
• AC 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: measurability/agent-completable, user/problem present … anti-slop; plus triggered overlays … and a bounded broad pass' — lists ~12 sub-criteria as a single bullet; the observable outcome (what changes for the user/system when each is 'implemented') is not stated.
• SC 3: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that *measurably* help an agent revise the plan' — 'measurably help' is a subjective/post-hoc outcome with no in-session observable proxy defined.
• SC 4: 'sensible defaults shipped' — 'sensible' is subjective.

(b) Post-session-only validation:
• SC 5 / AC 8: 'REVIEW_RESULT … sufficient to reconstruct per-criterion FP/remediation analysis offline' — the actual reconstruction and FP/remediation rates are explicitly deferred to Follow-on F1 ('FP/remediation-rate tracking tooling'). The in-session check is therefore incomplete; the criterion as written implies offline analysis is the measure of sufficiency, which cannot be verified in-session.
• AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' — experiments are referenced externally (session log); no in-session step produces or validates these numbers. This is either already-done (undocumented) or a post-session dependency.

(c) Epic-of-epics scope (right-sizing):
• The single epic contains: a new event type + reducer skip + signing subsystem + LSP/ast-grep integration + syft+registry+LLM-fallback dep resolution + scc+lizard size tooling + ~12 Layer-1 criteria + ~13 Layer-2 criteria + criteria registry + generic orchestrator + claim integration + configurable chunking/routing/escalation + budget/checkpointing + docs. This is substantially larger than a single coherent-outcome deliverable and strains 'completable within ONE working session' severely.

(d) Non-durable / transition framing:
• SC 1: 'Claiming a ticket … requires a valid … attestation; absence … blocks the claim' — this is verifiable, but the criterion is about a gate being in place, which is durable. This passes the litmus test. ✓
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Split the epic into child tickets aligned to coherent single outcomes (e.g., Layer-1 tooling integration, Layer-2 criteria registry, attestation/signing, `claim` integration, observability sidecar). Each child ticket is then in-session completable.
2. Replace SC 3 ('measurably help') with an in-session observable proxy: e.g., 'every advisory finding in the JSON output has non-empty `criterion_id`, `location`, `finding`, and `severity` fields — verified by schema-validation test'.
3. Replace 'sensible defaults' with specific numeric values (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per LLM call, validated by the experiment recorded in experiments/chunking_calibration.md').
4. For the FP/remediation AC: either scope it to 'event schema is emitted and contains fields X, Y, Z — verified by unit test asserting event payload' and explicitly defer sufficiency-for-analysis to F1, or remove it from this epic's ACs.
5. Either include the chunking-experiment outputs in the plan or convert that AC to a child ticket with a concrete deliverable (a markdown results file + updated config defaults).

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: The plan defers the concrete values for chunking defaults, budget caps, and review depth to 'experiment results' and a linked session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') that the executing agent may not have access to. An agent implementing the gate cannot proceed without knowing these numbers — they directly affect AC items for 'per-plan budget cap', 'chunk-atomic checkpointing', and 'proportionate-depth routing'. The plan does not provide fallback heuristics, placeholder values, or a decision procedure if the session log is unavailable. This is a defaultable gap (minor) because an agent could pick conservative defaults, but it creates real ambiguity about what 'grounded in experiment results' means in practice.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the experiment-derived defaults (e.g., default chunk size = N criteria per call, budget cap = M tokens, depth tiers = low/medium/high at complexity thresholds X/Y) directly into the plan body or AC, or explicitly state a fallback rule ('if experiment results are unavailable, use these placeholder defaults: …') so the agent can proceed without access to the external session log.

findings.location: Design summary: 'heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND'; 'claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation'; AC: 'per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'; 'escalation by size+complexity+centrality'
findings.finding: The plan introduces significant new I/O and LLM compute paths (LSP/ast-grep calls, syft/registry dependency checks, scc/lizard sizing, multi-criteria LLM calls with chunking, optional model escalation) but states no time-bound or latency target for ANY of these operations — not even for the 'fast' claim-time attestation check. Specific gaps: (a) The hot-path claim check is described only as 'FAST' with no done-definition (e.g., p99 < 500 ms); (b) The out-of-band review has no wall-clock budget or timeout per tool invocation, only a token/compute 'budget cap' whose units are unspecified; (c) The tooling chain (solidlsp + ast-grep + syft + registry + lizard) involves multiple sequential I/O operations with no concurrency model, rate-limit handling, or per-tool timeout stated; (d) N+1 risk: criteria chunking iterates LLM calls over criteria sets — the plan mentions chunking is configurable but gives no guard against unbounded LLM calls (e.g., a ticket with 50 criteria, chunk size 1, triggers 50 serial LLM calls with no stated ceiling).
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated performance section or annotate relevant ACs with: (1) a latency SLO for the claim-time attestation check (e.g., 'must complete in < 1 s wall-clock on the hot path'); (2) a wall-clock timeout per Layer-1 tool invocation (e.g., 'each LSP/ast-grep call times out at T seconds → skip/pass'); (3) a maximum LLM-call ceiling per review run (e.g., 'no more than N chunk calls per plan regardless of criteria count'); (4) whether Layer-1 tool calls are parallelized or sequential, with a rationale.

findings.location: Multiple ACs: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented'; 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)'; 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'; Success Criteria: 'coaching findings … measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: Several acceptance criteria lack any concrete proving command or observable end-state check: (a) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria item 3) has no defined measurement — no metric, no benchmark, no comparison baseline, no test scenario. 'Measurably' is a hedge without a proving command. (b) 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec' (AC) — there is no described automated check or review rubric that an agent could run to confirm conformance; it would require human judgment or a gold-set comparison that is not described. (c) 'Defaults … grounded in the experiment results' (AC) — verifying this criterion requires access to the external session log; there is no described artefact (e.g., a config file with documented provenance, a test that asserts specific values) that proves the grounding. (d) The AC 'Docs: how the gate works …' has no completeness check — no doc outline, no list of required sections, no automated broken-link/presence test described. These are claimed outcomes with no proving step.
findings.suggested_edit: For each affected AC: (a) Replace 'measurably help' with a concrete proxy metric (e.g., 'in a test suite of N synthetic flawed plans, the gate surfaces at least X% of seeded defects'); (b) Add a rubric-check or gold-set evaluation for coaching-spec conformance (e.g., 'all findings in the test suite pass the grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist rubric, verified by automated field-presence checks'); (c) For experiment-grounded defaults, require a checked-in config with inline comments citing the experiment result that justifies each value; (d) For Docs, enumerate the required sections and add a CI step that asserts their presence.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)'; Design summary; Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria

Design summary: 'Two layers … LAYER 1 = a minimal, deterministic, structural floor'; 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp … syft … scc + lizard'; 'Execution: … per-plan budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing'; AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback)'
findings.finding:
The plan introduces multiple external failure points — LSP server calls, ast-grep subprocess invocations, syft + package-registry HTTP calls, scc/lizard process invocations, and LLM API calls — as well as stateful writes (attestation signing, REVIEW_RESULT event emission, checkpointing). The fail-open policy for tool crashes/timeouts is explicitly stated for Layer-1 tooling, but error handling for the following is absent or unaddressed:

1. **LLM call failures** (rate-limit, timeout, malformed response): the plan mentions budget caps and chunk-atomic checkpointing, but says nothing about retry/backoff or graceful-degradation semantics when an LLM call fails mid-review. It is unclear whether a failed chunk yields INDETERMINATE, blocks, retries, or silently skips criteria.
2. **Package-registry HTTP calls** (syft + registry fallback): fail-open is mentioned for unsupported languages but not for transient network errors on supported languages (e.g., registry returns 500 or times out mid-review).
3. **Attestation/REVIEW_RESULT event writes**: the plan states these are sidecar events, but does not address idempotency — if the signing step partially succeeds (event written but signature not recorded, or vice versa), re-running the review could emit duplicate REVIEW_RESULT events or produce a stale/corrupt attestation head-state.
4. **Checkpoint durability**: 'chunk-atomic checkpointing' is named but not specified — it is unclear whether checkpoints are written atomically, what happens on crash between chunks, or how a resumed run avoids double-counting completed chunks.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a reliability section (or expand the 'Execution' bullet) covering: (a) LLM call retry/backoff policy and the exact semantics of a chunk failure (→ INDETERMINATE? retry N times? escalate?); (b) explicit fail-open treatment for transient registry/network errors on supported stacks (not just unsupported languages); (c) idempotency guarantee for REVIEW_RESULT event emission (e.g., event keyed on review-run-id so re-runs are safe); (d) checkpoint atomicity spec (write-then-rename, or equivalent) so a crashed review can resume without re-running completed chunks or producing duplicate verdicts.

findings.location: Title states this is an 'epic'; Design summary lists 10+ design dimensions; Acceptance Criteria lists 12 bullet items spanning criteria registry, orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, routing, checkpointing, feedback authoring, sidecar events, config, docs, and experiment-grounded defaults; Follow-on work lists F1–F12
findings.finding: The ticket is explicitly an epic and acknowledges it has follow-on children (F1–F12). However, the plan does not state whether the 12 AC items map to already-filed child tickets, or whether this epic is itself the single leaf ticket an agent is expected to execute in one session. If children exist and are tracked elsewhere (the session log reference `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` is mentioned but not linked), the decomposition may be sound. If this is a leaf ticket, it is far too large: it crosses ≥3 layers (event store, orchestrator, LLM runner, LSP/tooling, claim flow, CI integration, config, docs), introduces multiple new interfaces (criteria registry, orchestrator, REVIEW_RESULT event type, attestation schema), and carries 12 heterogeneous AC items — well beyond single-session scope. The plan references 'children' routing logic (has_children + complexity) internally, but does not declare its own children. Without access to the linked session log or a child-ticket listing, decomposition adequacy cannot be confirmed.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit 'Child tickets' section listing the filed (or to-be-filed) breakdown of this epic into executable leaf tickets, or clarify in the description that this is an epic-level planning document and that execution happens through separately tracked children. If children are already filed, link them directly rather than relying on the session log reference.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Attestation: signed result … The cert is the composable CI rigor signal'; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'; 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event with finding fingerprints'
findings.finding:
The plan introduces a signed attestation artifact and a `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event containing per-criterion verdicts and finding fingerprints. These are new data paths, but the plan does not classify their access level or sensitivity:

1. **Attestation signing**: the signing mechanism is referenced but the key management approach (who holds signing keys, how keys are stored, rotation policy) is not mentioned. If the signing key is stored alongside the codebase or in a poorly protected location, it could be forged or leaked.
2. **REVIEW_RESULT event**: the event captures 'per-criterion verdicts + finding fingerprints + metadata + override justifications'. If findings contain excerpts of code or ticket descriptions (which could include PII or secrets from ticket content), the event's storage and access controls are unspecified.
3. **--force override with justification**: justification strings written to event log — no mention of whether these are sanitized or access-controlled.

However, there is no clear indication that this system handles user PII, exposes new HTTP endpoints, or transmits data externally in a way that would demand full OWASP Top-10 analysis. The security surface is primarily internal tooling. The ambiguity lies in whether the signing key and REVIEW_RESULT event store are subject to existing rebar access controls (likely, but unconfirmed without codebase access).
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief security note covering: (a) attestation signing-key storage and rotation (e.g., 'key stored in rebar's existing secrets store, same controls as close-gate signing'); (b) access classification for REVIEW_RESULT events (internal-only, same ACL as other ticket events); (c) confirmation that finding fingerprints do not capture raw secret/credential content from ticket descriptions.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Layer-1 and Layer-2 AC bullets; Success Criteria bullet 3
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan at all. There is no mention of how the gate itself will be tested: no unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g., what happens when solidlsp is unavailable, when the ticket has no AC section, when the DAG is cyclic), no integration or contract tests for the signing/attestation path, no tests for the `claim` fast-check integration, no failure/timeout/error-path tests for the polyglot tooling calls, and no tests for the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event emission. The plan does require a 'fail-open' guarantee for unsupported stacks — a property that is only verifiable by explicit tests — but no test covering that path is named. The coaching spec (AC bullet 7) is itself a criterion the plan applies to agents, yet the plan applies none of it to its own verification work. At minimum, the following are absent: (a) any happy-path test, (b) any failure/timeout path test (solidlsp crash → skip, registry unavailable → fallback), (c) any boundary test (oversized plan, empty AC section, non-Latin file paths), (d) any TDD RED test with a named dependency edge, (e) any test that the signed attestation is rejected after a material edit.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing plan' subsection to the AC (or as a separate AC bullet) that at minimum names: (1) unit tests for each Layer-1 check with both pass and fail-open paths; (2) an integration test for the attestation invalidation-on-material-edit path; (3) a contract test for the `claim` fast-check (valid cert → proceed, absent/stale cert → block); (4) a test confirming `REVIEW_RESULT` is emitted but reducer-ignored; (5) a boundary test with an unsupported language to confirm fail-open. TDD: for each new Layer-1 checker, name the RED test (e.g., `test_readiness_shape_missing_ac_header_fails`) before the implementation bullet.

findings.location: Context section, paragraph 1; Success Criteria bullets 2–3
Acceptance Criteria — all 12 AC bullets; Success Criteria bullet 6; AC bullet 11 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding:
Several acceptance criteria are not in-session completable or measurably evaluable as written:

(a) AC bullet 11: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — the experiment work is external and not scoped in this ticket. Whether defaults are 'grounded' in results is not evaluable from repo artifacts alone; the linked session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` is not a repo artifact.

(b) AC bullet 3: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: … plus triggered overlays (router-selected) and a bounded broad pass' bundles at least 15 distinct sub-criteria into one bullet. As a unit it is not right-sized for one session; an agent cannot deterministically verify it is 'complete' without an explicit enumeration of all 15 criteria and a pass/fail state per criterion.

(c) AC bullet 7: 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec' — 'conforms' is subjective without a declared rubric or a deterministic check. The coaching spec itself is described in prose; there is no machine-checkable or PR-CI-checkable artifact named.

(d) Success Criteria bullet 3: 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' — this is a post-sprint outcome (requires observing downstream agent behavior) and is explicitly deferred to follow-on F1/F10. It should not appear as a success criterion for this ticket.

(e) AC bullet 6: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing … centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' — the measurable outcome is not stated; 'configurable' and 'computed' are effort-framed without observable end-states (e.g., a config schema, a test fixture with expected routing output).
findings.suggested_edit: For each affected AC bullet: (a) Replace AC 11 with 'Default values for chunking/budget/depth are declared in a config schema and match the values recorded in experiment artifact <specific path or ticket reference>'; (b) Enumerate Layer-2 criteria explicitly (numbered list) so completeness is binary; (c) Replace 'conforms to coaching spec' with 'each advisory finding emitted in tests includes criterion_id, location quote, severity, and suggested_edit fields (validated by a schema test)'; (d) Move 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' to follow-on F1 and replace with an in-session proxy ('in the review result fixture, each advisory finding includes a grounded location and a suggested_edit'); (e) For AC 6, state the observable: 'a config file with documented keys is committed and routing logic is covered by a unit test asserting correct depth selection per complexity tier'.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)', 'Attestation', '`claim` integration', 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'; AC bullet 1, 4, 5, 6
findings.finding:
The overlay applies: the plan introduces (a) a new pipeline stage at open→in_progress touching the existing `claim` command, (b) new cross-component dependencies on solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, and rebar.llm, (c) a new attestation/signing sub-system that extends the close-gate signing, and (d) a new `REVIEW_RESULT` event type that the reducer must explicitly ignore.

(a) Coupling risk: The `claim` integration and the new dependency on rebar.llm extension points are noted but the coupling interface is not defined (e.g., what contract/protocol does `claim` call into the review gate via?). Without an explicit interface boundary, changes to `claim` or rebar.llm could silently break the gate.

(b) Changeability: Several thresholds/defaults are mentioned ('generous initial budget', 'per-plan budget cap', 'default set EMPIRICALLY') but the plan defers grounding them to linked experiment work outside the repo. There is no reference to a config schema that makes these values changeable without code edits.

(c) Documentation/ADR: The plan says 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' — a session log is not a committed repo artifact. The AC includes a 'Docs' bullet (last AC) covering config/cert/CI-check but does not mention an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry for the architectural decision to add a new gate stage and signing sub-system. Whether a living ADR is planned cannot be determined from the plan as written; hence AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the AC: (a) a bullet naming the interface boundary between `claim` and the review gate (e.g., 'a typed Python protocol/abstract base class defines the ReviewGate.check() contract, and claim calls only that interface'); (b) explicit mention that the config schema is committed as a versioned file and all budget/chunking/depth defaults are declared there; (c) a bullet requiring an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the architectural decision to add this gate stage and its signing sub-system.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Two concrete design decisions are deferred to experiment results that are not included in the plan: (1) default chunking size (criteria-per-LLM-call), and (2) default per-plan budget cap and depth posture. The plan instructs an executing agent to ground these defaults in 'experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and a session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b'), neither of which is inline. An agent cannot set these values without access to those external artefacts. Under a benign reading the linked session log may be accessible to the executing agent, making this a minor rather than major gap — but the plan should not rely on an external session log for in-scope design decisions.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline at least the order-of-magnitude decisions: e.g. 'default chunk size = N criteria per call; default per-plan token budget = X; depth tiers: leaf/simple = shallow, leaf/complex = medium, container = deep'. Mark these as 'subject to experiment refinement' so the agent has a concrete starting point without blocking on external data.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation; the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND.'; AC: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing; escalation by size+complexity+centrality; per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new I/O and LLM-compute paths (LSP calls via solidlsp, ast-grep invocations, syft + registry lookups, scc + lizard runs, multiple LLM calls per criteria chunk, attestation signing), yet supplies no time-bounded done-definitions or latency targets for any of them — not even for the 'FAST check' on the hot `claim` path. Specific gaps: (a) 'claim is a FAST check' has no numeric target (e.g. p99 < 500 ms); (b) the out-of-band review has no wall-clock SLO stated anywhere, only a vague 'per-plan budget cap' in tokens — token budget ≠ latency bound; (c) polyglot tool calls (LSP startup, registry HTTP, lizard) are each individually unbounded; the plan says 'fail-open on crash/timeout' but never names the timeout value; (d) the multi-LLM-call path has no stated concurrency model (sequential vs parallel chunks), making N+1 call patterns undetectable from the plan; (e) 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' introduces a graph traversal whose complexity is unstated.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a performance AC or sub-section with: (1) `claim` fast-path latency target (e.g. ≤ 200 ms p99, attestation signature check only, no LLM); (2) per-tool timeout defaults (e.g. LSP = 10 s, registry HTTP = 5 s, lizard = 30 s); (3) out-of-band review wall-clock cap (e.g. ≤ 5 min for shallow, ≤ 15 min for deep); (4) chunk parallelism strategy (e.g. 'chunks run concurrently up to pool size P'); (5) centrality computation complexity note (e.g. 'BFS bounded to depth D or K nodes').

findings.location: Multiple ACs; Success Criteria section; Design summary
findings.finding:
The plan is largely a high-level design narrative with a list of acceptance criteria, but almost no described implementation steps that map to those criteria. The E6 two-step check reveals the following concrete gaps:

(a) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' — no proving command or observable is described; 'see linked experiment work' is not a step that produces evidence.

(b) 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)' — the AC states a quality property but names no concrete check: no eval harness, no rubric test, no golden-set comparison. 'Conforms' is a hedge without a measuring instrument.

(c) 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented … measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria SC-3) — 'measurably help' is a claimed outcome with no described measurement: no A/B setup, no baseline metric, no acceptance threshold. This is a red-flag hedge.

(d) 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event … offline-analysis-ready' — 'offline-analysis-ready' is a quality claim with no proving command (no schema, no replay script, no sample query).

(e) 'Docs: how the gate works …' — the AC covers documentation but no step describes what constitutes done (e.g. which doc pages, reviewed by whom, or a doctest/link-checker command).

(f) 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check' — no end-to-end test scenario or command is described that would prove the gate actually blocks an unsigned claim and accepts a signed one.
findings.suggested_edit: For each AC that claims a behavioral or quality property, add a concrete proving command or observable. Examples: (b) 'run eval suite on N golden plan/finding pairs; ≥ X% of findings rated grounded+specific by rubric'; (c) 'record revision-needed rate before/after; target Y% reduction'; (d) 'schema test: REVIEW_RESULT event round-trips through offline replay script with no data loss'; (f) 'integration test: attempt claim without attestation → blocked; attempt with valid attestation → succeeds; attempt with stale attestation → blocked'. Remove or replace the phrase 'measurably help' unless a measurement plan is added.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Attestation' bullet; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding … (extends the close-gate signing)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage (open→in_progress gate) and explicitly extends the close-gate signing contract ('extends the close-gate signing'). It crosses at least two component boundaries: the claim command, the review orchestrator, and the existing completion-verifier / signing subsystem. No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is listed among the acceptance criteria or deliverables for these cross-component coupling points. The 'Docs' AC covers user-facing documentation (how the gate works, config, fail-open, cert semantics, CI-check pattern) but does not mention capturing the architectural decision about attestation-contract extension or the new REVIEW_RESULT event type in a developer-facing design record. This cannot be confirmed without seeing the repo's existing ADR/CLAUDE.md conventions — hence AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC item: 'An ADR or CLAUDE.md section captures: (a) the decision to extend the close-gate signing contract for plan-review attestations, (b) the new REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event schema, and (c) the coupling between the claim command and the out-of-band review orchestrator.' This keeps the architectural decision durable and reviewable.

findings.location: Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; AC: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'
AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'; Design summary: 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'
findings.finding:
Two execution-blocking ambiguities exist, though neither is an outright FAIL given the benign-reading filter:

1. **Experiment-grounded defaults**: The AC requires 'defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' and references 'linked experiment work', but no experiment results, numbers, or ranges appear in the plan body. An executing agent cannot derive or validate these defaults without the referenced session log, which may or may not be accessible. If the session log is treated as a first-class design input available to the agent, this is merely MINOR; if not, the agent would have to choose arbitrary values.

2. **Session log as plan-of-record**: The plan states 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' for ~30 research reports and v4 design decisions. The self-contained summary is intentional, but several AC items (e.g. the exact Layer-1 criterion list, the REVIEW_RESULT event schema fields, the complexity/centrality routing thresholds) depend on detail only present in that log. If the log is inaccessible or ambiguous on any of these points, the agent faces a clarification-stop.

These are flagged AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL because there is a plausible reading where the session log is fully accessible and acts as a supplemental spec, making the plan executable.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline the key numeric defaults (initial budget cap, chunk-size default, depth-routing thresholds, complexity scoring formula) into the AC or an appendix table, so the plan is self-contained. At minimum, add a note indicating whether the session log is a required input artifact for the executing agent or background context only.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Attestation' bullet; 'claim integration' AC; 'Criteria registry' AC; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event' AC
findings.finding: The plan crosses at least four component boundaries: (1) the existing completion-verifier / close-gate signing infrastructure is extended ('extends the close-gate signing'); (2) the `claim` command path is modified to add a fast attestation check; (3) a new `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event type is introduced into the event/reducer pipeline; (4) rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points are reused. Each of these is a genuine cross-component coupling point. Sub-check results: (b) PASS — thresholds/config knobs (chunking, budget caps, per-criterion blocking, depth) are explicitly called out as configurable project knobs. (c) AMBIGUOUS — the plan says 'full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' and references a 'plan-of-record v4', but it is not clear whether a durable ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is a required deliverable of this epic. The Docs AC covers user-facing documentation ('how the gate works, config surface, fail-open behavior, cert semantics, CI-check pattern') but does not explicitly require capturing the novel architectural decisions (new event type, new pipeline stage, extension of signing infrastructure) in an ADR or equivalent internal design record. Sub-check (a): the cross-component dependencies are acknowledged and motivated in the Design summary but no explicit interface/event boundary mitigation is cited for the `claim`-path coupling beyond 'fast signed-attestation check'. Whether an existing interface boundary already covers this is not determinable without codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC or Success Criterion: 'An ADR (or equivalent design-doc update) capturing the new pipeline stage, event schema, signing-extension, and rebar.llm integration contract is committed alongside the implementation.' This makes the architectural documentation a reviewable deliverable rather than something implicitly delegated to the session log.

findings.location: Context section; Success Criteria SC-3; Design summary — 'Coaching / feedback spec' bullet
Multiple locations (see finding)
findings.finding:
The plan is substantially executable without clarification, but three gaps could cause an agent to pause or make a consequential implicit assumption:

1. **'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'** (AC, last bullet before Docs) — the actual experiment results and derived defaults are not present in the plan; they live in session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b`. An executing agent must either access that log (assumed available) or choose defaults without grounding. If the log is inaccessible, this becomes a blocking ambiguity.

2. **'Escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality'** (Design summary — Execution bullet; AC — Configurable chunking bullet) — the escalation thresholds (e.g., at what size/complexity score does escalation trigger, which 'stronger model' is the target) are not specified. The plan says these are configurable, but no default value or derivation path is given, meaning an agent must either invent a default or guess from the session log.

3. **'Triggered overlays (router-selected)'** (AC — Layer-2 advisory criteria) — the overlay selection logic (what triggers which overlay, and the full list of overlays beyond the one named in T5e) is not defined in the plan body. An agent implementing the router would need to make design decisions not captured here.

These are all defaultable or resolvable via the referenced session log, so severity is minor rather than major, but they do represent deferred design choices that could cause the agent to make inconsistent or unintended decisions.
findings.suggested_edit: For (1): inline the derived defaults (or a table of them) directly in the AC rather than citing the session log, e.g. 'default chunk size = N criteria per call; default budget cap = $X; default depth = shallow/deep per complexity score'. For (2): specify the escalation threshold in the AC or config-defaults section, e.g. 'escalate when complexity_score > T or file_count > N; target model = <name or config key>'. For (3): enumerate the defined overlay types and their trigger conditions in the AC or a referenced sub-ticket.

findings.location: Design summary (all sections); Acceptance Criteria (12 distinct bullet items); Follow-on work (F1–F12)
findings.finding: This epic-scale plan clearly signals it needs children but the plan body contains no decomposition into child tickets. The AC covers at least 6 independent implementation concerns: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria + polyglot tooling, (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching spec, (3) criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) attestation + signing + invalidation, (5) `claim` integration, (6) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip + observability substrate, plus (7) configurable chunking/routing/escalation and (8) docs. Each of these touches different files, different subsystems (event model, LLM runner, LSP/ast-grep tooling, CI-check surface), different layers (data/storage, orchestration, external tools, API surface), and in several cases introduces a new interface (criteria registry contract, sidecar event schema, attestation signing protocol). The plan acknowledges the epic's size only by deferring F1–F12 to follow-on tickets, but makes no mention of decomposing the epic itself into linked child tickets that would be right-sized for single-session execution. The plan's own 'Design summary' says 'routing pivots on has_children + complexity' and the routing logic specifically keys on `has_children`, implying the authors know decomposition matters — yet the epic has no children of its own. Without children, an agent picking up this ticket is expected to execute >12 acceptance criteria spanning multiple layers and 3+ new interfaces in a single trajectory, which contradicts the plan's own stated motivation ('too big to one-shot' is ~44% of agent failures).
findings.suggested_edit: File a set of linked child tickets, one per major concern cluster (e.g., child-1: Layer-1 structural floor + polyglot tooling; child-2: Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching feedback spec; child-3: criteria registry + orchestrator; child-4: attestation + signing + invalidation; child-5: `claim` integration; child-6: REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer; child-7: chunking/routing/escalation config; child-8: docs + CI-check pattern). Update this epic's description to reference those children and confirm coverage of all 12 AC items.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all 12 bullets; Success Criteria; Design summary
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan at any level of specificity. None of the 12 AC items name a test, a test type, a test file, a test scenario, or a RED/GREEN TDD step. There is no mention of: (a) how to test the Layer-1 deterministic floor (e.g., a fixture ticket with a missing `## Acceptance Criteria` section that must produce a BLOCK verdict); (b) failure/edge paths for the polyglot tooling (LSP crash → fail-open, unsupported language → skipped=PASS, malformed plan → no exception); (c) boundary scenarios for the attestation (stale cert on material edit, `--force` override path, cert bound to wrong commit); (d) the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event (reducer-ignored property is asserted, not just declared; event schema is validated); (e) Layer-2 advisory findings (a coaching output checked for grounded/specific/severity-ranked shape); (f) the `claim` integration fast-path (valid cert → proceeds, absent cert → blocks, stale cert → blocks). The plan's own framing—'coaching review emits findings that help an agent revise'—is itself an outcome that should have an observable, measurable test (e.g., 'given a plan with ambiguous AC, Layer-2 returns ≥1 finding with severity ≥ minor grounded in a specific AC item'). Without any test specification, an implementing agent has no RED tests to anchor implementation and no way to confirm that fail-open, signing, or sidecar-event behaviors are correct.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing plan' section (or augment each AC bullet) that names: (1) at least one fixture-driven Layer-1 test per check (e.g., missing AC header → BLOCK, LSP unavailable → PASS/skipped); (2) attestation lifecycle tests (fresh cert passes, material-edit invalidates, --force records override); (3) a reducer-skip integration test confirming REVIEW_RESULT events are ignored by state reconstruction; (4) a Layer-2 output shape assertion (finding has criterion_id, severity, location, suggested_edit); (5) a `claim` fast-path unit test (valid/absent/stale cert). Mark each with a declared dependency edge on the relevant implementation AC.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution' section: 'Review depth scales with complexity + has_children; escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality'; 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. claim is a FAST check'; 'Polyglot tooling: solidlsp + ast-grep + syft + registry + LLM-fallback'; 'per-plan budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new I/O and LLM/compute paths (solidlsp calls, ast-grep invocations, syft + registry lookups, multiple LLM calls per criterion chunk, model escalation), yet provides no time-bounded done-definitions for the hot-path `claim` check or the overall out-of-band review. Specifically: (a) **Latency**: The `claim` path is described only as 'FAST' with no numeric target (e.g., <500 ms, <2 s).
