The out-of-band review has a 'per-plan budget cap' but no wall-clock target or p95 SLA. An agent trajectory that blocks waiting for a slow review has no basis for timeout decisions. (b) **N+1 / redundant calls**: The plan describes per-criterion LLM calls chunked by a configurable knob, but does not state whether criteria sharing the same file context are batched or whether the same file/symbol is fetched from LSP once and cached. A naive per-criterion LSP call pattern is O(criteria × files). (c) **Scalability**: Registry lookups (package existence) are unbounded — no rate-limiting, retry policy, or fallback timeout is stated beyond 'LLM-fallback'. Concurrent `claim` calls on a large project with many tickets are not addressed. The plan acknowledges the tension ('claim is a FAST check; heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND') but does not resolve it with concrete targets, making it impossible for an agent to implement a correct timeout or resource-limit boundary.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a performance section (or augment the 'Execution' AC bullet) with: (1) a wall-clock SLA for the `claim` fast-path (e.g., <1 s, attestation lookup only, no LLM); (2) a p95 wall-clock target for the out-of-band review at small/medium/large complexity tiers; (3) a statement that LSP/ast-grep results are fetched once per review and shared across criteria chunks (not per-criterion); (4) a per-registry-call timeout + retry cap (e.g., 5 s, 2 retries, then LLM-fallback); (5) a note that concurrent `claim` calls serialize on the attestation read, not the review computation.

findings.location: AC list (12 items): Layer-1 criteria, Layer-2 criteria, criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, chunking/routing/escalation/budget, feedback authoring, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event, per-criterion config, bug exemption, experiment-grounded defaults, docs — all in one ticket; Success Criteria section (6 items); Follow-on work list (F1–F12).
findings.finding: This epic spans at minimum 5–6 independent vertical concerns (Layer-1 deterministic engine, Layer-2 advisory engine, criteria registry/orchestrator, attestation/signing subsystem, claim-gate integration, observability sidecar) plus cross-cutting concerns (polyglot tooling, config surface, docs, experiment-grounded defaults). It clearly exceeds the 'single concern' and '>6 success criteria' thresholds that the criterion flags as requiring children. The plan acknowledges epic scope and lists F1–F12 follow-ons, but the AC does NOT reference any child tickets that decompose this epic into executable leaf tickets — leaving it ambiguous whether children exist (filed but not linked) or are absent entirely. Without children, an agent attempting to execute this as a single unit cannot coherently do so in one session. The plan cannot be verified as correctly decomposed without access to the linked session log or any child ticket registry.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly list or link the child tickets that correspond to each major vertical slice (e.g., 'CHILD-1: Layer-1 deterministic criteria engine', 'CHILD-2: Layer-2 advisory engine', 'CHILD-3: Attestation/signing subsystem', 'CHILD-4: claim-gate integration', 'CHILD-5: REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + observability', 'CHILD-6: Config surface + docs'). If children are already filed, add their IDs to the epic description. If they are not yet filed, add an explicit AC item: 'Children tickets are filed and linked before any child is claimed.'

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all 12 AC items; Success Criteria section; no dedicated testing section present.
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan whatsoever. There is no mention of unit tests, integration tests, or end-to-end tests for any of the following new behaviors: (a) the Layer-1 deterministic checks (e.g., what happens when LSP/ast-grep crashes, times out, or returns malformed output — the 'fail-open' guarantee is stated as a design intent but no test verifies it); (b) the Layer-2 advisory review pipeline (no tests for the LLM call path, chunking, checkpointing on budget exhaustion, or INDETERMINATE outcome); (c) the attestation signing and invalidation logic (no tests for stale-signature detection, material-vs-non-material edit classification, or signature verification); (d) the claim-gate fast-path check (no tests for missing attestation, stale attestation, or --force override flow); (e) the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event (no tests that the reducer correctly ignores the event type). Failure and edge paths are especially absent: malformed ticket inputs, non-Latin content, oversized plans hitting budget caps, concurrent claims, tool unavailability. The criterion also requires that changed behaviors get corresponding test work — the claim (open→in_progress) path is being modified and no existing-test modification plan is noted.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated 'Testing plan' section (or AC items) covering: (1) Unit tests for each Layer-1 check with at least one happy-path and one fail-open (tool crash/timeout → PASS) case per check; (2) Unit tests for attestation signing, staleness detection, and material-edit classification; (3) Integration test for the full open→in_progress claim flow: no attestation → blocked; stale attestation → blocked; valid attestation → allowed; --force override → allowed + justification logged; (4) A test asserting the reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT event type without state mutation; (5) At least one boundary/adversarial scenario per layer (e.g., empty description, 10 MB plan, non-Latin AC text, LLM returning malformed JSON).

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution' paragraph: 'Generous initial budget + depth posture'; 'Configurable chunking'; 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality'; 'out-of-band' heavy review; AC item 6: 'per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing and an INDETERMINATE outcome'.
findings.finding:
The plan introduces substantial new I/O and LLM/compute paths, triggering this overlay. Three concrete gaps:

(a) LATENCY — The plan states the heavy review runs 'out-of-band' and the claim check is 'fast', but provides no time-bounded done-definition for either path. 'Fast' and 'generous budget' are not observable targets. For an agent workflow where the open→in_progress transition is on the critical path of development, an unbounded out-of-band review could block indefinitely. No wall-clock SLO (e.g., 'claim check < 200 ms; full review completes within N minutes or emits INDETERMINATE') is stated.

(b) RESOURCE EFFICIENCY / N+1 LLM CALLS — The plan describes per-criterion LLM calls chunked into groups, plus a 'bounded broad pass', plus optional escalation to a stronger model. For a ticket with many criteria (the AC lists 20+ criteria across L1+L2) this could become an O(criteria_count) LLM-call pattern per review with no stated bound on total calls or cost. The 'per-plan budget cap' is mentioned but the unit (tokens? dollars? calls?) and the cap value are unspecified.

(c) SCALABILITY — No concurrency or rate-limit handling is described. If multiple agents claim tickets simultaneously, the review pipeline could fan out unbounded LLM requests. No pool size, queue depth, or back-pressure mechanism is mentioned.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit performance AC or sub-section covering: (1) A wall-clock SLO for the claim fast-path (e.g., '< 500 ms p99 — only signature verification, no LLM call') and a soft timeout for the out-of-band review after which INDETERMINATE is emitted; (2) Define the budget cap unit (e.g., 'per-plan LLM token budget cap: configurable, default X tokens; chunk count ≤ ceil(criteria/chunk_size) with a hard max of Y chunks'); (3) State concurrency behavior: 'review tasks are queued; max N concurrent LLM requests per rebar process; additional claims queue or receive INDETERMINATE immediately'.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Attestation' bullet; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'; AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a signed attestation artifact and a `claim` integration endpoint/command that checks that attestation. It is unclear whether the signing key material, the attestation-check path, and the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event are accessible only to authenticated/internal callers or are reachable by arbitrary agents or CI processes without auth. The plan states the cert is 'checkable by an external CI process' (SC #6) but does not declare the access level (public/authenticated/internal-only) of that check path. Similarly, it is not documented whether the attestation payload could leak sensitive ticket contents (e.g. file paths, architecture notes) to unauthenticated readers. These omissions are AMBIGUOUS — the existing rebar auth model may already cover these paths, but that is not stated in this plan. Per OWASP A01 (Broken Access Control) and A02 (Cryptographic Failures), the access classification and data-protection posture of the new signing/check surface should be explicit.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Security boundaries' note to the Attestation section: (a) declare the access level of the claim-check endpoint (internal-only / authenticated CI token / public); (b) confirm the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event does not include raw ticket text or secret-adjacent content, or note what is redacted; (c) state what protects the signing key (e.g. rebar's existing signing infra, no new surface).

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)'; 'Execution' bullet (chunking, per-plan budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing); AC: 'Configurable chunking … per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing and an INDETERMINATE outcome'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly adds multiple failure points: LLM calls (Layer-2 advisory review), external tool calls (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, registry lookups, lizard/scc), and stateful write operations (REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event write, attestation signing/write). The plan mentions 'chunk-atomic checkpointing' and 'fail-open' for tools, and INDETERMINATE as a non-blocking outcome for budget exhaustion — these are reliability mechanisms. However, the plan does not describe: (a) retry/backoff/circuit-breaker behavior for external registry lookups or LSP server calls (tool crash → skip is documented, but transient network failure vs hard crash is not distinguished); (b) idempotency of the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar write and the attestation event write — if a write is retried after partial commit, what prevents a duplicate event or a corrupt attestation; (c) recovery path when chunk-atomic checkpointing itself fails mid-chunk. It is AMBIGUOUS whether the existing rebar.llm infrastructure already provides retry/backoff for LLM calls (it may), but that is not stated here and not clearly extended to the new tool calls.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Reliability' sub-section (or inline notes) covering: (1) LLM call retries — state whether rebar.llm already handles this or if the orchestrator adds it; (2) external tool transient-failure handling — distinguish crash/timeout (skip=PASS) from transient network error (retry N times, then skip); (3) idempotency guarantee for REVIEW_RESULT and attestation writes (e.g. event is content-addressed / deduplicated by fingerprint so a retry is safe); (4) what happens when checkpointing itself fails (partial chunk state — is it discarded or resumed?).

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; AC: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria … measurably help an agent revise the plan'; Success Criteria #3 'measurably help an agent revise'
findings.finding:
Two acceptance/success criteria fail the in-session measurability check:

(a) SC #3 / AC Layer-2 statement: 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' — 'measurably help' is a post-sprint outcome (requires observing agent behavior over multiple runs and comparing revision rates), not something verifiable in-session via a repo artifact or deterministic command. There is no observable proxy defined (e.g. 'findings include grounded citations and suggested edits for ≥N% of FAIL verdicts', which would be in-session checkable). This is effort/outcome-vague.

(b) AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — 'grounded in experiment results' is not in-session verifiable unless the experiment results are available as a committed artifact in the repo. The criterion as written depends on an external session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) and linked experiments that are not part of this epic's deliverables. An agent executing this ticket cannot demonstrate in-session that the chosen defaults are properly grounded without access to those results.

All other ACs appear to describe concrete code artifacts, event schemas, or configuration surfaces that are in-session checkable (implemented and testable against the repo).
findings.suggested_edit: (a) Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a specific in-session proxy, e.g.: 'Every FAIL/AMBIGUOUS finding in the coaching output includes a non-empty `location` (quoting the plan) and a `suggested_edit` (when confidence ≥ threshold), verifiable by inspection of the output schema and at least one integration test fixture.' (b) For the defaults AC: either commit the experiment results as a referenced artifact in the repo (e.g. `docs/experiments/plan_review_sizing.md`) so the grounding is checkable in-session, or reframe as 'Defaults are documented in `config/defaults.yaml` with rationale comments referencing the experiment log ID'.

findings.location: AC list (all 12 bullet points); Success Criteria (all 6 bullets); Design summary 'Execution' and 'Attestation' sections
findings.finding:
The plan describes a rich set of deliverables but does not specify any concrete proving commands or end-to-end verification procedures for most of them. Specific gaps:

(a) No proving command for the core gate behavior: there is no described `pytest`/`rebar` command or integration-test scenario that would demonstrate the open→in_progress transition is actually blocked when attestation is absent or stale. 'Claiming a ticket … requires a valid … signed plan-review attestation' is stated as a success criterion but no test or command proves it in-session.

(b) Layer-1 'fail-open' behavior: 'fail-open on any unsupported language/tool (skipped = pass)' is asserted but there is no described test fixture or command that would demonstrate a tool crash results in PASS rather than FAIL.

(c) 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event' (AC #8): the reducer skipping this event type is a critical behavioral claim, but no proving command (e.g. a reducer test, a schema validation) is described.

(d) 'Attestation … invalidation-on-material-edit' (AC #4): the plan states material edits invalidate the cert, but no test or verification scenario is described for this behavior.

(e) 'Docs: how the gate works …' (AC #12): documentation existence is verifiable, but the criterion gives no location (file path, doc section) where it would live, making it unmappable to a proving step.

(f) The plan contains several hedged phrasings: 'sensible defaults shipped' (SC #4) and 'sufficient to reconstruct … analysis offline' (SC #5) are not tied to a concrete proving command or test.

The union of described steps (design bullets, AC list) does not close the gap to a verifiable end-state because no step includes a `how do we know it works` artifact.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Verification plan' section (or inline per-AC notes) mapping each acceptance criterion to a concrete proving command or test artifact. Minimum examples: (1) `pytest tests/gate/test_claim_gate.py` — asserts open→in_progress is blocked without valid attestation; (2) `pytest tests/gate/test_layer1_failopen.py` — asserts tool crash yields PASS verdict; (3) `pytest tests/events/test_reducer.py::test_review_result_ignored` — asserts reducer skips REVIEW_RESULT; (4) an integration fixture showing material-edit invalidation. Also replace hedged terms ('sensible defaults', 'sufficient to reconstruct') with testable assertions (e.g. 'defaults validated by experiment fixture X', 'REVIEW_RESULT schema validated by JSON Schema file Y').

findings.location: Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; Design summary: 'COACH agents to author better plans'; Success Criteria SC-3: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings…that measurably help an agent revise the plan'

Design summary: 'extends the close-gate signing'; 'Criteria registry…reusing rebar.llm extension points'; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'; 'claim integration'; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new cross-component coupling points — a new gate wired into the claim flow, a new sidecar event type wired into the reducer (with a special 'reducer-ignored' carve-out), a signing subsystem that 'extends' the close-gate, a new criteria registry reusing rebar.llm extension points, and a new centrality/blast-radius computation at plan time — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is mentioned for any of these architectural decisions. The AC for docs covers user-facing docs ('how the gate works, the config surface…') but omits the architectural decisions (event schema, reducer contract, signing extension contract, orchestrator extension points). Changeability is partially addressed (chunking/budget/depth are configurable knobs), but the criteria registry's extension contract and the sidecar event schema are not declared as versioned/evolvable interfaces. This is a new pipeline stage with no doc/ADR, matching the MAJOR threshold.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC such as: 'An ADR (or CLAUDE.md section) documents: (a) the new REVIEW_RESULT event schema and why the reducer ignores it; (b) the signing-extension contract shared with the close-gate; (c) the criteria-registry extension-point contract; (d) the centrality/blast-radius computation integration point and its data source.' Also verify the criteria-registry schema and sidecar-event shape are declared as versioned interfaces so they can evolve without a breaking change.

findings.location: Design summary: 'signed attestation'; 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'
findings.finding: The plan does not add user-facing endpoints, data storage of PII, secrets handling, or auth boundaries beyond an internal cryptographic signing mechanism already mirrored from the close-gate. The signing mechanism is an internal integrity check, not a new access-classified endpoint. No new data transmission of sensitive material is introduced. Security overlay is not applicable.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (11 ACs listed); Follow-on work (12 follow-on items explicitly deferred); Design summary; Success Criteria (6 items)
findings.finding: The ticket is explicitly structured as an epic with a well-reasoned scope. The plan acknowledges its large scope and explicitly carves follow-on work (F1–F12) into separate linked tickets, demonstrating active decomposition discipline. The epic's success criteria (6 items) and AC (11 items) are coherent around a single concern — the plan-review gate — with no unrelated goals mixed in. Bugs, FP-tracking tooling, CI helpers, and auto-demotion are all deferred. The plan also notes routing/chunking/depth are configurable knobs, signaling the authors are aware of execution-session sizing concerns. As an epic, children are expected to be filed as separate tickets per the follow-on section; the plan does not enumerate child tickets inline, but this is acceptable for an epic-level description.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all 11 ACs; Success Criteria — all 6 SCs; Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)'
findings.finding: The plan describes a sophisticated system with many failure modes (tool crash/timeout → fail-open, stale attestation invalidation, INDETERMINATE budget-exceeded outcome, chunking failures, LSP/ast-grep unavailability, LLM errors during criteria evaluation, --force override path, material-edit invalidation) but the AC contains no testing plan whatsoever — no mention of unit tests, integration tests, contract tests, regression tests, or even a stated test strategy. The AC for Layer-1 says 'fail-open' and 'fail-open IRONCLAD' but no test verifies that a tool crash actually returns PASS (not FAIL or an exception). The attestation invalidation logic (material vs. non-material edits) has nuanced boundary conditions with no test coverage declared. The --force override path, INDETERMINATE outcome, and sidecar event schema are likewise untested per the plan. The docs AC mentions behavior but not testability. The 'Defaults…grounded in experiment results' AC references experiments but does not specify how the defaults will be validated in test. This is a new user-facing flow (claim integration) with only implicit happy-path coverage — a MAJOR gap.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC covering the testing plan, for example: 'Test coverage includes: (a) Layer-1 fail-open: tool crash/timeout/unsupported-lang all return PASS (not FAIL/exception) — parametrized per tool; (b) attestation invalidation: material edits (description/AC/file_impact) invalidate; non-material edits (tags/comments) do not; (c) claim integration: missing attestation blocks, stale attestation blocks, valid attestation passes, --force+justification is logged and succeeds; (d) INDETERMINATE outcome: budget-exceeded emits non-blocking result; (e) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar: reducer skips the event type without error; (f) per-criterion config: advisory criterion does not block; blocking criterion blocks with --force override available.'

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; AC: 'child coverage + consistency (containers)'; Follow-on: 'F7 cross-ticket no-overlap / dependency-aware semantic detection'
findings.finding: Three gaps require an executing agent to make unguided design decisions: (1) The AC states defaults are 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' but the session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) is not accessible to an agent executing this plan — the actual numeric defaults (chunk size, budget cap, depth thresholds) are not stated anywhere in the plan itself, so an agent must either discover them from an external artifact or choose them arbitrarily. (2) The criteria registry AC lists Layer-2 criteria by name but does not specify how many criteria constitute one LLM call at the default chunking setting, leaving the 'chunk finer / coarser' knob without a concrete starting value for implementation. (3) 'Centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' references a computation whose data source (dependency graph? import graph? an existing rebar primitive?) is not identified, requiring the agent to investigate or invent it. These are defaultable gaps (not hard blockers) but they will cause the agent to pause or make arbitrary choices in the implementation. All three are MINOR in isolation; the experiment-results dependency is the most likely to cause a real stop.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline the key defaults from the session log into the AC or Design Summary: e.g., 'Default chunk size: N criteria per LLM call (frontier), M (local/small); default budget cap: $X per plan; default depth tiers: simple leaf / complex leaf / container.' Also specify the data source for centrality computation (e.g., 'uses the existing import-graph primitive in rebar.analysis, falling back to file-count if unavailable').

findings.location: Design summary — 'Attestation' bullet; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'; AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a signed attestation, a `claim` enforcement path, and a `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event — all of which involve data storage and transmission within rebar's event log. The plan does not classify the access level of the `claim` endpoint/command or the `REVIEW_RESULT` event (public/authenticated/internal-only), and does not address whether the signing key material (used to produce the attestation) is treated as a secret (no-log, secured storage). The attestation is also described as a CI-readable signal, implying external-facing consumption. These touch auth boundaries and data-transmission concerns. However, rebar appears to be a CLI/internal tool; if attestation signing keys and the event log are entirely local/filesystem-scoped with no network transmission or PII involved, several concerns would be not-applicable. This cannot be determined from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short security-boundary note to the Attestation and `claim` sections: (1) classify the `claim` check path (internal-only CLI invocation, no network endpoint); (2) state how the signing key is stored/sourced (env var, secrets manager, local keyring) and that it must not be logged or embedded in the `REVIEW_RESULT` event; (3) note whether the `REVIEW_RESULT` event contains any PII or sensitive plan content that requires access control.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution' bullet ('Generous initial budget + depth posture'); AC: 'per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing and an INDETERMINATE outcome'; AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback)'
findings.finding: The plan adds multiple failure points: external LLM calls (Layer-2 review), LSP/ast-grep tooling invocations, syft + registry lookups (network), and stateful write operations (attestation signing, `REVIEW_RESULT` event emission). The plan partially addresses reliability via: fail-open on tooling crashes/timeouts (IRONCLAD), chunk-atomic checkpointing, INDETERMINATE outcome for budget exhaustion, and LLM-fallback for deps. However, the following gaps are not addressed: (a) **Error handling for the registry/network lookup** in package-existence checks — the plan names 'syft + registry + LLM-fallback' but does not specify retry/backoff/circuit-breaker behavior for the registry call; the 'LLM-fallback' covers unavailability but not transient errors. (b) **Idempotency of the `REVIEW_RESULT` event write** — if the agent crashes mid-emission or after partial write, the plan does not state whether re-running produces a duplicate event or safely overwrites/deduplicates. (c) **Attestation signing write** — no mention of what happens if signing fails partway (partial signature stored vs. clean rollback). These are MAJOR for (b) and (c) per the criterion because they are writes with no stated idempotency guarantee. (a) is at least partially mitigated by the LLM-fallback but is still ambiguous about transient retries.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the AC or Design summary: (1) 'Registry/network lookups use exponential backoff with N retries before falling back to LLM; timeout T seconds constitutes a skip (fail-open).' (2) 'REVIEW_RESULT event emission is idempotent: a second emission for the same review-run ID replaces the first (or is deduplicated by the reducer-ignored handler).' (3) 'Attestation signing is atomic: a failed signing attempt leaves no partial signature and the claim gate sees absence-of-cert, not a corrupt cert.'

findings.location: Success Criteria SC-3 ('Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'); AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding:
Two criteria fail the measurability/in-session-completability test:

(a) **SC-3 — 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'**: The word 'measurably' implies a quantified outcome (e.g., reduction in plan-defect rate), but no in-session observable proxy is defined — no unit test asserting finding format, no fixture asserting grounding/severity fields, no CLI output contract. As written this is post-sprint-only validation (multi-session FP/remediation analysis, which is explicitly deferred to follow-on F1/F10). This makes the criterion effort-framed and post-sprint-only — MAJOR per the criterion.

(b) **AC 'Defaults … grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'**: The proving evidence ('experiment results') lives in an external session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) that the agent cannot verify in-session. There is no artifact in the repo (a config file, a doc table, a test) that would let a reviewer confirm the defaults are empirically grounded. This is a post-session dependency with no in-repo observable. MAJOR.

All other ACs reference concrete repo artifacts (Python implementation, attestation signing logic, event schema, config surface, docs) and are evaluable in-session.
findings.suggested_edit:
(a) Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a concrete in-session proxy, e.g.: 'Layer-2 review output conforms to the coaching spec: each advisory finding includes non-empty criterion_id, location, finding, severity, and suggested_edit fields (validated by a unit test against fixture tickets).' Defer the empirical 'measurably improves plans' claim to follow-on F10.

(b) Replace 'see linked experiment results' with: 'Defaults are documented in `config/plan_review_defaults.md` with a table of (chunk_size, budget_cap, depth_threshold) values and the rationale; a CI test asserts the shipped defaults match the documented values.' This makes it in-repo and in-session verifiable.

findings.location: Multiple ACs; Success Criteria; Design summary — 'Execution' and 'Attestation' bullets
findings.finding:
Several acceptance criteria lack a concrete proving command or in-repo artifact that would produce evidence of completion, and there are gaps between the described steps and the stated end-state:

(a) **AC 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec'** — no proving command. 'Conforms' is verified how? No mention of a test, schema validation, or example output fixture. This is a hedge-free claim with no evidence path. MAJOR.

(b) **AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'** — the experiment results are in an external session log. No in-repo artifact or deterministic command can verify this criterion is met. MAJOR.

(c) **AC 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'** — 'reusing extension points' has no proving command. A test asserting the orchestrator passes through rebar.llm's catalog/runner API would suffice, but none is specified.

(d) **SC-6 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process'** — no example CI command, no schema, no documented check format. An external process can't verify what it can't see defined. The claim is aspirational without a concrete artifact (e.g., `rebar verify-attestation <ticket_id>` with exit-code semantics documented).

(e) The plan notes the full design lives in session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b`, but that log is not a repo artifact. Several criteria implicitly depend on it for their definition of 'done' (e.g., Layer-2 criteria list, coaching spec). If the log is unavailable to the executing agent, those criteria have no in-session end-state definition.

Positively: Layer-1 criterion list, `claim` integration, `REVIEW_RESULT` event schema, and config surface ACs are concrete enough to be implementable and testable.
findings.suggested_edit:
(a) Add to AC: 'A unit test suite with ≥3 fixture plan reviews asserts that every emitted advisory finding has non-empty criterion_id, location, finding, and severity; a test asserts that a finding with no location fails the coaching-spec validator.'
(b) See F1 suggestion above — document defaults in a repo artifact.
(c) Add: 'An integration test invokes the orchestrator via rebar.llm's runner interface and asserts the result schema matches the criteria registry contract.'
(d) Add: 'A `rebar verify-attestation <ticket_id>` subcommand (or equivalent) exits 0 if a valid, non-stale attestation exists, 1 otherwise; exit-code semantics and the attestation JSON schema are documented in `docs/plan_review_gate.md`.'

findings.location: Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; Success Criteria; Design summary: 'coaching agents to author better plans'

Design summary: 'Criteria registry + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'; 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new cross-component integration points — a criteria registry/orchestrator reusing rebar.llm, a new sidecar event type (REVIEW_RESULT) in the reducer pipeline, a new signing/attestation mechanism extending the close-gate, and a hook into the `claim` command — yet no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is called out in the acceptance criteria or follow-on work. The AC entry for the criteria registry says 'reusing rebar.llm extension points' but does not acknowledge or document how this coupling is justified and mitigated (e.g., via a defined interface/contract). The attestation signing extension to the close-gate is similarly undocumented as an architectural decision. The Docs AC item covers user-facing docs (how the gate works, config, CI-check pattern) but does not include an internal ADR or architectural decision record for the new pipeline stage and cross-component contracts.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC or sub-task: 'An ADR (or equivalent CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) is authored capturing: (a) the coupling contract between the plan-review orchestrator and rebar.llm; (b) the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event schema and its reducer-skip contract; (c) the attestation signing extension to the close-gate signing mechanism.' This can be a lightweight decision record, not a formal document.

findings.location: Design summary and AC — no new endpoints, PII handling, or auth boundaries introduced
findings.finding: The plan adds no new HTTP endpoints, data storage, PII handling, or external-facing auth boundaries. The attestation signing is internal to the rebar system and does not expose a network surface. This overlay criterion is not applicable.

findings.location: Epic structure; Follow-on work section (F1–F12); AC list
findings.finding: This is an explicitly scoped epic with a well-structured decomposition rationale. The follow-on items (F1–F12) are deliberately deferred and justified. The plan notes 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)' and carves out 12 distinct follow-on threads, indicating the author has thought carefully about scope boundaries. The epic has children implied (Layer 1 criteria, Layer 2 criteria, orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, observability) and the AC maps to distinct deliverable clusters. While the AC list is broad (12 items), each maps to a coherent sub-system of the gate feature, and the plan explicitly calls for children tickets for follow-on work. No single-concern failure is evident — the 'and' joining Layer 1 + Layer 2 is architecturally necessary (they are the two layers of the same gate). Acceptable for an epic-level plan.

findings.location: AC section — all 12 AC items; Success Criteria; Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)'
findings.finding: The plan describes a sophisticated, multi-layered system but contains no testing plan whatsoever. None of the 12 acceptance criteria mention tests, test coverage, or how correctness of the gate itself will be verified. Specific gaps: (a) No failure/edge-path coverage: the fail-open behavior for unsupported languages/tools ('unsupported lang / no server / crash / timeout → skipped = PASS') is a critical invariant but no test for it is specified. (b) No boundary scenario coverage: oversized tickets, malformed plan descriptions, tickets with no AC section, stale attestation invalidation edge cases — none are called out. (c) The Layer-1 deterministic checks (readiness shape, file/symbol resolution, package existence, oversize signals, task-DAG validity) each have failure modes that need unit tests, yet the AC only says 'implemented in Python.' (d) The attestation invalidation logic (material-edit-commit binding) is security-relevant and requires explicit test scenarios (e.g., edit after signing, non-material edit does not invalidate). (e) The `claim` integration fast-path check needs a test that a missing or stale attestation actually blocks. No TDD red-test declarations are present. The plan defers experimentation ('defaults grounded in experiment results') but gives no test harness for those experiments.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC item: 'A test plan (unit + integration) covers: (a) each Layer-1 deterministic criterion with at least one PASS and one FAIL fixture; (b) fail-open behavior for each tool (mock crash/timeout → PASS result); (c) attestation invalidation on material edit and non-invalidation on non-material edit; (d) claim blocks on missing/stale attestation and allows with valid attestation; (e) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event is emitted and skipped by the reducer. Tests are isolated from live LSP/registry calls via mocks/fixtures.' Also explicitly note whether TDD is expected for the deterministic Layer-1 checks.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB... default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Three areas carry deferral language that an executing agent cannot resolve without external inputs: (1) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — the experiment results are referenced but not summarized, so an agent cannot select concrete defaults without running or consulting those experiments first. If the experiments are a prerequisite, this is a hidden dependency. (2) 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data' — 'generous' is undefined (no token/call/time unit). (3) 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b' — if this log is unavailable to the executing agent, they lose the plan-of-record v4 detail, which is described as the authoritative design source. These are defaultable/minor given the epic scope, but warrant flagging.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the key experiment conclusions (concrete chunking defaults, budget cap numbers) into the AC or add an explicit prerequisite: 'Experiment work (linked ticket) must be completed and its results summarized in this ticket before implementation begins.' For 'generous budget,' specify a concrete starting value (e.g., 'initial budget cap: N LLM calls per review, configurable') so the agent has a concrete baseline.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)'; Design summary; Acceptance Criteria

Design summary: 'claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation; the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND'; 'Execution: chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB'; 'Generous initial budget + depth posture'; 'per-plan budget cap'
findings.finding: The plan introduces substantial new I/O and LLM/compute paths (multiple LLM calls per review, LSP/ast-grep tool invocations, syft + registry lookups, scc + lizard runs, signing operations) and explicitly splits the path into a fast 'claim' check and a heavy out-of-band review. However: (a) Latency — no time-bound is stated for the 'FAST check' on the hot claim path; 'fast' is subjective and not defined in milliseconds or relative to any baseline. (b) Resource efficiency — 'generous initial budget' is mentioned without a concrete ceiling; the criteria-per-LLM-call chunking knob is described but no default number or Big-O characterization is given for how many LLM calls a review of N criteria produces. (c) Scalability — concurrency handling, rate-limit treatment, and what happens when the out-of-band reviewer is already busy (queue depth, starvation) are not addressed. These cannot be confirmed as sound without seeing the referenced experiment results (AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results') which are deferred.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the Design summary or a dedicated Performance section: (1) an explicit latency SLO for the synchronous claim-path check (e.g. '< 200 ms p99'); (2) a Big-O statement for LLM calls per review (e.g. 'O(ceil(|criteria|/chunk_size)) LLM calls'); (3) a stated queue/concurrency model for the out-of-band runner (e.g. max parallel reviews, timeout, back-pressure). Reference or inline the experiment-derived defaults in the AC rather than deferring them entirely.

findings.location: Success Criteria SC-3: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; AC: 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)'
findings.finding: Several criteria are not in-session verifiable as written: (a) SC-3 uses 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' — 'measurably' implies empirical comparison data (before/after plan quality) that cannot be produced within one working session from repo artifacts alone; no proxy observable (e.g. 'all findings include a grounded criterion reference and a suggested edit') is substituted. (b) The AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' is explicitly deferred to linked external experiment work; an agent cannot verify this criterion in-session because the experiment artifacts are not part of the deliverable scope here. (c) AC 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec' is verifiable in principle (inspection of emitted findings) but lacks a concrete proving command or checklist that could be run against the implementation — 'conforms' is subjective without a machine-checkable rubric. These make the criteria effort-framed or post-sprint-only.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' in SC-3 with a concrete in-session proxy, e.g. 'every advisory finding includes a criterion_id, a quoted location, a severity, and (when confidence > 0.7) a suggested edit — verifiable by inspecting the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event schema and a sample run.' For the experiment-defaults AC, either bring the experiment work into scope or reframe as 'defaults are documented with rationale in config; values are placeholders pending experiment results filed as [linked ticket].'

findings.location: AC list; Success Criteria list; Design summary; Follow-on list
findings.finding: Overall coherence is strong — every Success Criterion maps to at least one AC and the described design. Two potential gaps worth flagging: (1) The Design summary prominently describes an 'INDETERMINATE' verdict outcome (budget/tool unavailable, non-blocking by default, project-configurable). The AC mentions 'INDETERMINATE outcome (non-blocking default, configurable)' only within the escalation/chunking AC bullet — it is not separately called out as a first-class deliverable with a verifiable behavior. This risks it being implemented incompletely without a dedicated coverage check. (2) The AC 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)' is stated in both the AC list and the Follow-on section (F6), which is near-duplicative; however, it is present in the AC as a testable negative behavior, so this is minor. No orphan criteria or uncovered deliverables were found beyond these. Terminology is consistent throughout ('Layer-1/Layer-2', 'REVIEW_RESULT', 'attestation', 'material event').
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated AC bullet for the INDETERMINATE outcome: 'When a review cannot complete due to budget exhaustion or tool unavailability, the outcome is recorded as INDETERMINATE in the REVIEW_RESULT event; claim is not blocked by default; project config can promote INDETERMINATE to blocking.' This gives the implementing agent a concrete, verifiable target.

findings.location: Success Criteria SC-1 through SC-6; AC list broadly; Design summary: 'Attestation: signed result'; 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan describes end-states richly but largely omits concrete proving commands or in-repo verification steps for its own acceptance criteria: (a) SC-1 ('Claiming a ticket requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation; absence or a material-edit-invalidated signature blocks the claim') — no proving command is described (e.g. 'rebar claim <ticket> with/without attestation returns exit-code X and message Y'). The AC bullet mirrors the SC but adds no test/command. (b) SC-2 ('Layer-1 floor blocks only on sound, unambiguous checks and fails open') — 'fails open' is asserted but no test strategy for the fail-open path is described (no 'given unsupported language → check skips and attestation records SKIPPED for that criterion'). (c) SC-6 ('signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process') — no example CI command or schema reference is given; 'checkable' is hedged without a proving artifact. (d) The AC 'Docs: how the gate works…' is a deliverable with no verification step (no 'docs pass a doc-linter' or 'docs include a worked example'). Several ACs contain the word 'implemented' (e.g. 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python') which is effort-framed, not outcome-observable. The gap between 'what we'll do' (implement criteria, emit events, sign attestations) and 'what done looks like' (specific commands that prove each SC) is significant.
findings.suggested_edit: For each SC/AC, add a one-line proving command or artifact. Examples: SC-1: 'Verified by: `rebar claim TICKET_ID` without attestation exits non-zero with message containing "plan-review attestation required".' SC-2: 'Verified by: running Layer-1 against a ticket with an unknown language; REVIEW_RESULT sidecar shows criterion status = SKIPPED.' SC-6: 'Verified by: `rebar attest verify <sig_file>` exits 0 in a CI script example in docs.' Replace 'implemented' in AC bullets with the observable outcome (e.g. 'Layer-1 criteria are invokable and produce structured PASS/FAIL/SKIPPED results persisted in the REVIEW_RESULT event').

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard'; 'Attestation: signed result'; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event'; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit'
findings.finding: The plan adds multiple external failure points and stateful write operations but addresses reliability unevenly: (a) Error handling for individual tool calls — LSP server crash/timeout is addressed ('crash / timeout → skipped = PASS'), but no equivalent is stated for syft registry lookups (network failure, rate-limit, HTTP 5xx), LLM provider outages during the out-of-band review, or signing failures. A signing failure is particularly dangerous: if the sign step fails after a completed review, is the review result lost? Is retry safe? (b) Idempotency of the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event write — the plan says the reducer ignores the event type, but does not state whether emitting the event twice (e.g. retry after a partial write) is safe. Duplicate sidecar events could corrupt offline FP/remediation analysis. (c) The 'chunk-atomic checkpointing' is mentioned as a feature but no recovery path is described: if a chunk completes but the checkpoint write fails, does the agent re-run that chunk (idempotent?) or skip it (possibly losing findings)? (d) Material-edit invalidation of the attestation — if invalidation logic fails (e.g. the commit-binding check crashes), does the gate fail open or closed? This is a security-adjacent reliability question not answered by the plan.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Reliability section (or expand the existing design) covering: (1) LLM provider error → INDETERMINATE outcome, no retry storm (bounded retries with backoff); (2) Registry/syft failure → criterion skipped (same fail-open as tool crash), logged in REVIEW_RESULT; (3) Signing failure → review result persisted but not certified, agent is told to re-run sign step (idempotent); (4) Sidecar event write is idempotent (deduplicated by review_id + chunk_id); (5) Checkpoint write failure → chunk re-runs from last durable checkpoint (idempotent criteria evaluation); (6) Material-edit invalidation check failure → fail closed (attestation treated as stale), with --force override.

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and SC: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: Two concerns: (1) The AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' defers its own completeness to external experiment work not defined in this ticket. If the experiment results do not yet exist or are not committed in-repo, this criterion cannot be verified in-session. (2) The SC bullet 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' uses a post-session validation frame ('measurably') — measuring whether coaching findings actually help requires observing agent remediation rates over time (Follow-on F1), which is explicitly deferred. The in-session proxy (e.g., 'findings conform to coaching spec') is implied but not stated as the check. The remaining ACs and SCs are concrete, deterministic, and repo-verifiable (event schema, signing, LSP/ast-grep integration, config knobs, etc.), and are right-sized for an epic-level ticket.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with 'conform to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/severity-ranked/affirming/checklist-form), verifiable by review of emitted findings'. For the experiment-results AC, either inline the defaults or add a sub-ticket reference and note that the AC is satisfied if defaults are documented and justified in config/docs, even if experiments are still pending.

findings.location: AC: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children); escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)'; AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'; Design summary: 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB ... default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Three defaultable gaps that stop short of being blockers but could cause an agent to pause: (1) 'Centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' — the algorithm or data source for centrality is unspecified. An agent would need to choose an approach (dependency graph? import graph? call graph?). (2) 'Reusing rebar.llm extension points' — the specific extension points are not named; an agent unfamiliar with the codebase would need to inspect it. (3) The experiment-derived defaults are deferred ('see linked session log'), so the agent must either run experiments first or pick interim values. None of these fully blocks execution (each has a plausible default path), but together they create stop-and-ask moments. The plan is otherwise highly detailed and unambiguous in its two-layer model, verdict model, routing logic, and fail-open behavior.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short note naming the centrality algorithm (e.g., 'import-graph in-degree via ast-grep') and the specific rebar.llm contracts the orchestrator should implement. For defaults, state interim values explicitly (e.g., 'chunk size = 3 criteria/call until experiments complete').

findings.location: N/A
findings.finding: The plan does not introduce new user-facing endpoints, PII handling, external data transmission, or new auth boundaries. The attestation/signing mechanism is an internal rebar event; the 'CI-checkable signature' is a read of an existing event field, not a new exposed endpoint. No OWASP Top 10 surface is introduced. Overlay not applicable.

findings.location: AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'; AC: 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event ... reducer skips the type'; AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit'; Design summary: 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity'
findings.finding: The plan introduces at minimum four new cross-component integration points: (a) a new orchestrator reusing rebar.llm contracts, (b) a new sidecar event type that the reducer must explicitly skip, (c) a new signing/invalidation mechanism extending the close-gate signing, and (d) a new routing/escalation sub-system. None of these carry an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc reference. The plan references a session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) as the 'plan-of-record', but this is not a committed, discoverable architectural decision record. Specifically: the reducer change (skipping REVIEW_RESULT) is a coupling point that any future reducer developer must know about; the signing extension must not diverge from the close-gate signing contract; the rebar.llm orchestrator reuse implies a contract that needs to be stable. Without a doc/ADR capturing these decisions, future maintainers cannot safely evolve the system. On changeability: the plan explicitly notes configurable thresholds (chunking, budget, depth, per-criterion blocking), which is good. No hardcoded soon-to-change values are visible.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC: 'An ADR or CLAUDE.md section is committed that documents: (1) the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event contract and why the reducer skips it, (2) the signing/invalidation extension to the close-gate pattern, (3) the rebar.llm orchestrator interface the criteria registry plugs into, and (4) the centrality/blast-radius computation approach.' Reference this doc from the criteria registry and attestation ACs.

findings.location: Design summary: 'heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND'; AC: 'Configurable chunking ... per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'; AC: 'escalation by size+complexity+centrality'; SC: 'Claiming a ticket (open→in_progress) requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation'
findings.finding: The plan correctly separates the fast `claim` check (attestation presence) from the heavy out-of-band review, which is a sound latency decision. However, three performance-relevant paths lack time-bounded done-definitions or stated resource limits: (1) The out-of-band review pipeline makes multiple LLM calls (per-criterion, per-chunk, plus a broad open-ended pass, plus potential escalation to a stronger model). No wall-clock target or total-call-count ceiling is stated beyond 'per-plan budget cap' — the budget unit (tokens? dollars? calls?) is unspecified. (2) Layer-1 tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) involves I/O against potentially large repos. Fail-open is specified for crashes/timeouts, but no per-tool timeout value is given. Without a stated timeout, 'fail-open on timeout' has no teeth. (3) The escalation path ('stronger model by size+complexity+centrality') could trigger on every large ticket with no stated rate-limit or concurrency guard, creating an unbounded resource spike. These are not blocking the claim path (which is correctly fast), but the background review has no latency SLO and the tooling has no timeout budget, which means the 'out-of-band' promise is unenforceable.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the relevant ACs: (a) 'Each Layer-1 tool invocation has a configurable per-tool timeout (default: X seconds); timeout = skip = PASS.' (b) 'The per-plan budget cap is expressed in LLM call-count (or token budget); default = N calls/M tokens, configurable.' (c) 'Escalation to a stronger model is subject to the same per-plan budget cap and does not bypass it.' Concrete values can be placeholders pending experiment results, but the unit and mechanism must be named.

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design summary, all sections; SC and AC in full
findings.finding: Blind restate: The plan builds a two-layer gate that fires when an agent claims a ticket (open→in_progress), performs deterministic structural checks (Layer 1, blocking, fail-open) and advisory LLM-based coaching checks (Layer 2), produces a signed attestation, emits an observable sidecar event, integrates into the `claim` command, and is configurable per project. Comparison to title: The title promises a 'plan-review verification gate' at the open→in_progress transition. The plan delivers exactly that — no material scope drift in either direction. All six success criteria map to observable gate behaviors. The explicit exclusion of bugs (routed to follow-on) is a scoping decision, not a contradiction. Callers of `claim` are acknowledged (the fast attestation check). The follow-on list is clearly bounded and not load-bearing for this epic's success criteria.

findings.location: Success Criteria SC-3: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Two criteria are not fully evaluable in-session. (1) SC-3 uses 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' — 'measurably' implies a quantitative outcome but no observable in-session metric is defined (no delta-pass-rate, no eval harness, nothing a CI run can assert). This reads as effort/outcome-vague rather than a specific observable end-state. (2) The AC bullet 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)' defers to an external artefact ('see linked experiment work') that is a follow-on, creating an implicit dependency the agent cannot resolve in-session if those experiments have not yet been run. The remaining AC/SC bullets are concrete and in-session evaluable (signing, sidecar event schema, claim integration, fail-open behaviour, config surface, docs).
findings.suggested_edit: Replace SC-3 with an observable proxy, e.g. 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings that conform to the coaching spec (grounded criterion ref, specific location quote, severity label, checklist form, suggested edit where confident), verifiable by reviewing output against a spec-compliance rubric in the test suite.' For the experiment-dependency AC, either inline the empirically-derived defaults as concrete values/ranges, or restate as 'Defaults are documented and rationale recorded in a design-note committed to the repo' to make it in-session verifiable.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution: chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; AC: 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'
findings.finding: Three pockets of deferred decisions could cause an agent to pause mid-execution. (1) Default chunking/budget/depth values are explicitly deferred to external experiment results not included in the plan ('see linked experiment work') — the agent must either guess or stop. (2) 'Centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' does not define the algorithm or data source (call-graph depth? file-import fan-in? something else?), leaving the implementing agent to choose an appropriate method. (3) The plan references session log '63cc-ec94-4792-464b' as the 'plan-of-record v4' but it is not reproduced or linked in a machine-accessible way, meaning design detail may be unreachable. These are defaultable gaps rather than complete blockers, hence AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL, but they could cause divergent implementations.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline concrete starter defaults (e.g. chunk size = N criteria per call, budget cap = $X, depth tiers) rather than pointing to external experiments. Define 'centrality' explicitly (e.g. 'import fan-in count from ast-grep; tickets touching files with fan-in > T are escalated'). Ensure the session-log reference is either reproduced inline or linked to a committed doc.

findings.location: N/A
findings.finding: The plan adds no new user-facing endpoints, no PII handling, no external data transmission beyond already-gated LLM calls, and no new auth boundaries. The attestation/signing mechanism is internal state management on an existing event bus. The T5c security overlay is not applicable.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'; AC: 'Criteria registry … + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points'; AC: 'reducer-ignored SIDECAR REVIEW_RESULT event … reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: Three maintainability concerns: (1) Coupling risk — The plan introduces a new orchestrator layer that reuses 'rebar.llm extension points' and couples to the existing close-gate signing mechanism ('extends the close-gate signing'), but neither the interface boundary nor the contract between these components is defined. Silently coupling a new pipeline stage to existing internals without a declared interface is the exact pattern the criterion flags. (2) Changeability — Multiple thresholds that are expected to evolve (complexity tier boundaries, budget caps, centrality fan-in cutoffs, per-criterion blocking defaults) are described as 'project knobs' but no configuration schema or default values are specified; without a schema, these could end up hardcoded or inconsistently scattered. (3) Documentation — The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (out-of-band review with signed attestation, sidecar event, criteria registry/orchestrator) but defers the ADR/design-doc to 'Docs' in the last AC bullet with no specified structure. The session-log reference is not a committed doc.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Define an explicit interface (protocol/ABC or event contract) between the new orchestrator and rebar.llm before implementation. (2) Ship a typed configuration schema (dataclass or Pydantic model) for all evolving thresholds alongside the implementation. (3) Add an explicit AC: 'An ADR or CLAUDE.md entry is committed that records the orchestrator/registry/signing architecture, the sidecar event schema, and the fail-open contract.' The final Docs AC bullet should reference this artefact specifically.

findings.location: Design summary — 'heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND'; 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation'; 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data; per-plan budget cap'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple I/O and LLM-heavy paths (Layer-1 LSP/ast-grep tool calls, Layer-2 multi-criterion LLM calls, registry lookups, sidecar event writes) yet provides no latency targets or resource bounds. Specific gaps: (a) Latency — 'claim is a FAST check' is stated for the hot path, but 'FAST' has no numeric bound (ms? s?). The out-of-band review path also has no wall-clock target, which matters for agent workflows where a stalled review blocks forward progress. (b) Resource efficiency — the chunked LLM call pattern is described but no maximum number of LLM calls per review, no token-budget ceiling per chunk, and no concurrency limit are stated. With N criteria and fine chunking, an O(N) LLM-calls-per-review pattern could emerge with no stated cap. (c) Scalability — 'per-plan budget cap' is mentioned but no value or formula is given; centrality/blast-radius computation at plan time could be O(files²) without a stated complexity bound or timeout.
findings.suggested_edit: Add concrete performance constraints: (a) 'claim attestation check completes in < 200 ms P99 (signature verification only, no LLM calls).' (b) 'Out-of-band review completes within T minutes wall-clock; if budget or time cap is exceeded, the outcome is INDETERMINATE.' (c) 'Maximum LLM calls per review = ceil(|criteria| / chunk_size); chunk_size ≥ 3 by default.' (d) 'Centrality computation is bounded to a BFS/DFS of depth ≤ D from touched files, with a configurable timeout T_c after which it is skipped (fail-open).'

findings.location: Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design summary; Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria
findings.finding: Blind restate: the plan builds a gate that fires when an agent transitions a ticket from open to in_progress, runs a two-layer review (deterministic structural checks + advisory LLM-based coaching), produces a signed attestation, integrates into the existing 'claim' command, and emits a sidecar observability event — all scoped to non-bug tickets. Comparison: this matches the title exactly. The plan does not drift into completion-verification (kept separate), does not under-deliver (all stated intent has a corresponding AC), does not contradict the goal, and explicitly acknowledges the existing close-gate that callers depend on (signing extends it rather than replacing it). Bugs are explicitly exempted with a rationale and follow-on reference. Intent fidelity is sound.

findings.location: Title + Design summary + Acceptance Criteria

Design summary: 'Trigger / enforcement: fires at open→in_progress. claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation; the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND.' / AC: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children); escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time); per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new I/O and LLM-heavy paths (LSP calls via solidlsp, ast-grep, syft/registry lookups, one-or-more LLM calls per criteria chunk, centrality/blast-radius computation at claim time) but provides no concrete latency targets or time-bounded done-definitions for the out-of-band review. 'FAST check' for claim is asserted but never quantified. Two specific gaps: (a) No wall-clock budget is stated for the out-of-band review path (e.g. 'must complete within N seconds/minutes'), making it impossible to validate whether the agent-facing experience is acceptable. (b) The chunk-per-LLM-call pattern is called out as a project knob but no worst-case call-count bound is given (e.g. 'at most K LLM calls per review'), leaving an O(criteria × chunks) call pattern without an upper bound. The 'per-plan budget cap' is mentioned but defined only in token/cost terms (implied), not in latency or call-count terms.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the AC or Design summary: (1) A concrete latency target for the claim fast-path (e.g. 'claim check completes in <500 ms; attestation lookup is a single signed-blob read'). (2) A worst-case LLM call-count bound for the out-of-band review (e.g. 'default config: ≤N LLM calls per review; configurable upper bound enforced by budget cap'). (3) An explicit timeout for the out-of-band review after which INDETERMINATE is returned.

findings.location: Success Criteria SC-3: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings … that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding: Two criteria fail the in-session completability / measurability tests: (a) SC-3 ('measurably help an agent revise the plan') is outcome-vague and post-sprint-only — measuring whether findings 'help' requires observing agent behaviour across future sessions; no in-session proxy or surrogate metric is defined (e.g. 'findings conform to grounded/specific/severity-ranked checklist format, verified by unit test or reviewer rubric'). (b) The AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)' defers its verification to an external session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') and linked experiment work that is not part of this epic's deliverables. There is no in-session command or artifact that proves the defaults are empirically grounded. If the experiment work is follow-on, this criterion cannot be evaluated within a single working session.
findings.suggested_edit: For SC-3: replace 'measurably help' with a format-level surrogate: e.g. 'each advisory finding references a criterion_id, a quoted location, a severity, and a suggested_edit field; conformance verified by schema validation in CI.' For the defaults AC: either (i) make the experiment work an explicit prerequisite/dependency of this epic with a deliverable artifact (e.g. 'experiment report doc committed to repo'), or (ii) reframe as 'defaults are documented with rationale in config schema; values are placeholders pending experiment results filed as follow-on.'

findings.location: AC item: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit + coverage record (extends the close-gate signing).' / SC-6: 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal.' / Follow-on F11: 'rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers'
findings.finding: Two coherence gaps found: (a) SC-6 states the attestation is 'checkable by an external CI process' as an in-scope success criterion, but Follow-on F11 explicitly defers 'CI integration helpers' to a separate ticket. The AC has no criterion covering how CI checks the attestation — only that the attestation exists. The described deliverable (CI-checkable signal) and its covering criterion are misaligned: SC-6 implies an end-to-end CI integration, but the AC only delivers the cert artifact. (b) The AC uses 'material-event-commit binding' while the Design summary uses 'latest MATERIAL event commit' — a minor terminology consistency issue, but the AC does not define what events are 'material' vs non-material (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition vs tags/comments/links/assignee), meaning a verifier cannot mechanically check this criterion without consulting the design body.
findings.suggested_edit: (a) Either move SC-6 to a follow-on (alongside F11) or add an AC item: 'A documented CI check command/API endpoint exists that returns pass/fail based on presence of a valid attestation, with a usage example in docs.' (b) Add an inline definition of 'material event' to the AC item on attestation, e.g.: 'material events: description, AC, file_impact, or decomposition edits; non-material: tags, comments, links, assignee changes.'

findings.location: AC items: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open: readiness shape … file/symbol/import resolution … package existence … oversize signals … task-DAG validity … AC/DD quality … destructive sniff.' / AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).' / SC-3: 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: Multiple acceptance criteria lack a concrete proving command or observable end-state check: (a) Layer-1 AC lists seven distinct checks (readiness shape, file/symbol resolution, package existence, oversize, task-DAG, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff) but no criterion describes how each is verified as correctly implemented — e.g. no mention of a test suite, integration test fixtures, or CI step that exercises each check with a known-bad plan and asserts a FAIL verdict. (b) Layer-2 AC lists ~12 advisory criteria similarly without a verifying artifact or test. (c) 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist)' — no proving command is described; 'conforms' is a hedge without a rubric test. (d) 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event … reducer skips the type' — the reducer-skip behaviour is stated but no test/assertion proving the reducer actually ignores the event type is described. The union of described steps does not close the loop to 'all criteria are verified working.'
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC item: 'A test suite (pytest or equivalent) exercises each Layer-1 check with at least one known-bad synthetic plan fixture and asserts the expected verdict (FAIL/PASS); Layer-2 advisory criteria are exercised with golden-set fixtures asserting finding structure conforms to schema. CI must pass these tests.' Also add: 'Reducer unit test asserts REVIEW_RESULT events are skipped (state unchanged).' For the coaching-spec conformance AC, add: 'A schema validator (JSON Schema or Pydantic model) enforces grounded/specific/severity-ranked/checklist structure on every emitted finding; CI runs this validator.'

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard. Unsupported lang / no server / crash / timeout → skipped = PASS, never FAIL.' / AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override.' / Design summary: 'chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple external failure points (LSP server, ast-grep, syft, package registries, LLM API calls) and stateful writes (attestation signing, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event emission, checkpointing). Reliability gaps: (a) The fail-open guarantee for tooling crashes/timeouts is stated ('IRONCLAD') but no retry/backoff/circuit-breaker mechanism is described for the LLM calls or registry lookups. 'Crash/timeout → skipped' describes the outcome but not the mechanism (e.g. is there a timeout value? a process-level wrapper?). (b) 'Chunk-atomic checkpointing' is mentioned but no recovery path is described: if the review process crashes mid-chunk, what state is durable and how is the review resumed without re-running completed chunks or emitting a duplicate REVIEW_RESULT event? (c) The attestation signing write: no idempotency guarantee is stated — if the signing step is retried (e.g. after a transient failure), could a duplicate or conflicting attestation be emitted? (d) '--force override' for claim: the AC mentions logging the override but does not describe how a race (two agents both forcing simultaneously) is handled.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the AC or Design summary: (1) 'Each external tool invocation is wrapped with a configurable timeout (default: Xs) and caught exception; on timeout or crash the criterion is marked SKIPPED.' (2) 'Checkpointed review state is keyed by (ticket_id, attestation_nonce); a resume loads completed chunk results from durable store and skips re-execution; duplicate REVIEW_RESULT events are prevented by idempotency key.' (3) 'Attestation signing is idempotent: signing the same (ticket_id, material-commit-hash) pair twice yields the same signature and does not create duplicate events.' (4) 'LLM API calls use exponential backoff with a max of N retries before recording INDETERMINATE for the affected chunk.'

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)', 'Execution', 'Attestation', 'Observability / FP substrate'; AC items 1, 4, 5, 6
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple significant failure points — external LLM calls (Layer-2 advisory review, escalation to a stronger model), LSP/ast-grep/syft/registry tool invocations (Layer-1), signing/attestation writes, and REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event writes — all of which require error handling and idempotency guarantees. The plan states 'fail-open' for tooling (unsupported lang/crash/timeout → skipped = PASS) but does NOT specify: (a) retry/backoff/circuit-breaker behavior for LLM calls or registry calls; (b) what happens if attestation signing fails mid-write (partial state safety / idempotency); (c) what happens if the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event write fails (dropped, retried, or corrupted state?); (d) whether chunk-atomic checkpointing is recoverable if the process is interrupted mid-chunk (the plan mentions 'chunk-atomic checkpointing' but gives no durability or recovery semantics). The fail-open guarantee for tooling is clearly stated, which is good, but the LLM call failure model and write-idempotency for the attestation and sidecar events are absent from both the design summary and all acceptance criteria. This cannot be confirmed without codebase access to see whether existing rebar.llm infrastructure already covers retry/backoff.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the design summary a dedicated 'Error handling & idempotency' subsection covering: (1) LLM call retry policy (e.g., exponential backoff, max retries, circuit-breaker threshold); (2) attestation write idempotency (what makes a re-issued cert safe if the first write partially completed — content-hash keyed?); (3) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar write durability (at-least-once vs at-most-once, and what a dropped event means for offline analysis); (4) chunk-atomic checkpoint recovery semantics (resume vs restart). Add a corresponding AC: 'All external calls (LLM, registry, LSP) have documented retry/backoff; attestation and REVIEW_RESULT writes are idempotent; checkpoint recovery is defined.'

findings.location: Context section; Success Criteria; Design summary — coaching posture paragraph
Epic structure; Follow-on work section (F1–F12); AC list
AC item: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'; Design summary — 'Execution' paragraph; Follow-on work F1
findings.finding: Two potential coherence issues: (1) The AC states 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' but neither the plan body nor any other AC describes commissioning or completing those experiments — they are referenced as if already done but there is no deliverable or criterion covering them. If the experiments are a prerequisite that must be completed first, they should be an explicit dependency or in-scope deliverable; if they are already complete, the plan should say so. (2) The AC mentions 'anti-slop' as a Layer-2 criterion in the AC list but the term 'anti-slop' does not appear in the design summary criteria descriptions, creating a minor terminology gap. These are not blocking but could leave the implementing agent uncertain about whether to run experiments or consume pre-existing results.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the AC or design summary whether the chunking/budget/depth experiments are (a) already complete and their results are available in the linked session log, or (b) must be run as part of this epic. If (a), add a note like 'Results are in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b; defaults are [X/Y/Z]'. If (b), add an explicit deliverable and covering AC. Also add 'anti-slop' to the Layer-2 criterion descriptions in the design summary for consistency.

findings.location: Success Criteria (all 6 items); AC items throughout; Design summary — 'Verdict model', 'Attestation', 'claim integration'
findings.finding: The plan describes an extensive set of deliverables and acceptance criteria but provides no concrete proving commands, end-to-end checks, or observable test outputs for any of them.
